group_by() doesn’t change how the data looks when printed — it tags the data frame as “grouped,” so that every subsequent summarise() (or mutate()) is computed separately within each group instead of across the whole dataset:
The result has one row per group — five rows for five counties — instead of summarise()’s usual single overall row.

title: 72  group_by()
text:
grouped <- group_by(df, grouping_column)
summarise(grouped, stat = <summary function>(column))
by_county <- group_by(participants, county)
summarise(by_county, mean_bp = mean(systolic_bp, na.rm = TRUE))

title: 72  group_by()
text:
72.2 Demonstration
library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

by_county <- group_by(participants, county)

county_summary <- summarise(by_county,
  n_participants = n(),
  mean_bp = round(mean(systolic_bp, na.rm = TRUE), 2)
)

county_summary
# A tibble: 5 x 3
  county      n_participants mean_bp
  <chr>                <int>   <dbl>
1 Cedar Falls             20  128.16
2 Millbrook               21  133.30
3 Oakford                 17  129.71
4 Riverdale               16  124.36
5 Springvale              26  131.46
Five rows, one per county — n() inside a grouped summarise() counts rows within each group, not the whole dataset.

title: 72  group_by()
text:
72.3 Explore
Try grouping by two columns at once: group_by(participants, county, smoker), then summarise(..., n = n()). Confirm the result has one row for every county-by-smoking-status combination that actually occurs in the data (not necessarily all 10 possible combinations, if some don’t occur).

title: 72  group_by()
text:
72.4 Solve the Problem
library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

by_county <- group_by(participants, county)

county_summary <- summarise(by_county,
  n_participants = n(),
  mean_age = round(mean(age, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
  mean_bp  = round(mean(systolic_bp, na.rm = TRUE), 2)
)

county_summary
# A tibble: 5 x 4
  county      n_participants mean_age mean_bp
  <chr>                <int>    <dbl>   <dbl>
1 Cedar Falls             20    50.58  128.16
2 Millbrook               21    48.00  133.30
3 Oakford                 17    42.20  129.71
4 Riverdale               16    51.27  124.36
5 Springvale              26    45.40  131.46
Millbrook has the highest mean blood pressure (133.30) of all five counties, while Riverdale has the lowest (124.36) — exactly the county-by-county comparison your professor asked for, computed in two group_by() + summarise() lines instead of five repeated subset-and-mean calls.

title: 72  group_by()
text:
72.5 Interpret the Result
Splitting participants by county and summarising within each group reveals real variation base R’s single overall mean (129.84 from the previous page) completely hid: Millbrook’s average blood pressure (133.30) is nearly nine points higher than Riverdale’s (124.36) — the kind of pattern a single ungrouped summary can never surface.

Common Mistakes

Forgetting group_by() alone doesn’t summarise anything. group_by(participants, county) just tags the data as grouped — printing it looks nearly identical to the ungrouped data frame. The grouping only takes visible effect once you call summarise() (or mutate()) afterward.
Leaving a data frame grouped after you’re done with it. If you group_by() and then later run mutate() for something unrelated, the mutation will unexpectedly happen within groups. Use ungroup() afterward if you need fully ungrouped data again.
Forgetting na.rm = TRUE still applies per group. Missing values are excluded within each group’s calculation, not just once overall — the rule from Level 2 still holds, just applied five times instead of once.

title: 72  group_by()
text:
72.6 Practice Problem
Use group_by() and summarise() to compute the number of participants and the mean bmi for each smoker status ("Yes" vs "No").

title: 72  group_by()
text:
72.7 Challenge Problem
Use group_by(participants, county) followed by summarise() to find which county has both the highest mean BMI and the highest proportion of smokers. Are they the same county?

title: 72  group_by()
text:
72.8 Key Takeaways

group_by(df, column) tags a data frame as grouped, without changing its printed appearance by itself.
Following group_by() with summarise() computes statistics separately within each group, producing one row per group.
Always ungroup() (or start a fresh pipeline) once you’re done with grouped operations, to avoid accidentally grouping later steps.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What does group_by(df, county) do by itself, before any summarise()?

Immediately computes county means
Tags the data frame as grouped by county, with no visible summary yet
Deletes all columns except county
Sorts the data frame by county

True or false. summarise() after group_by(df, county) produces one row per county.
Predict the output. If participants has 5 distinct counties, how many rows would summarise(group_by(participants, county), n = n()) return?
Find the error.
grouped <- group_by(participants, county)
grouped
# expecting to already see county-level means here



Show answers


b) Tags the data frame as grouped by county, with no visible summary yet.
True. Grouped summarise() computes one row per distinct group value.
5 rows — one per distinct county.
group_by() alone doesn’t compute anything — it only sets up the grouping. A summarise() call must follow to actually see county-level statistics.

title: 73  count()
text:
73.1 The R Concept
count() counts rows per group in one step — no group_by() needed first:
This is exactly equivalent to summarise(group_by(participants, county), n = n()) from the previous page, but in a single readable call. You can also count combinations of multiple columns at once, and sort by frequency with sort = TRUE:

title: 73  count()
text:
count(df, column)
count(participants, county)

count(participants, county, smoker)          # every county-by-smoker combination
count(participants, county, sort = TRUE)     # counts ordered largest first

title: 73  count()
text:
73.2 Demonstration
library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

count(participants, county)
       county  n
1 Cedar Falls 20
2   Millbrook 21
3     Oakford 17
4   Riverdale 16
5  Springvale 26
Note the column is automatically named n — matching exactly what table(participants$county) reported back in Level 2, just returned as a proper data frame instead of a table object.
count(participants, county, sort = TRUE)
       county  n
1  Springvale 26
2   Millbrook 21
3 Cedar Falls 20
4     Oakford 17
5   Riverdale 16
sort = TRUE reorders the rows largest-count-first, instantly surfacing that Springvale is the largest county in the study.

title: 73  count()
text:
73.3 Explore
Try count(participants, smoker) and confirm it matches Level 2’s table(participants$smoker) result (75 “No”, 25 “Yes”). Then try count(participants, county, smoker) — notice the result has one row for every county-and-smoking-status combination that actually appears, not all 10 mathematically possible combinations.

title: 73  count()
text:
73.4 Solve the Problem
library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

county_smoker_counts <- count(participants, county, smoker)

county_smoker_counts
        county smoker  n
1  Cedar Falls     No 17
2  Cedar Falls    Yes  3
3    Millbrook     No 15
4    Millbrook    Yes  6
5      Oakford     No 11
6      Oakford    Yes  6
7    Riverdale     No 12
8    Riverdale    Yes  4
9   Springvale     No 20
10  Springvale    Yes  6
All 10 county-by-smoking-status combinations appear (every county has at least one smoker and one non-smoker), giving your professor exactly the cross-tabulation table() alone (Level 2) could only produce for a single column at a time without extra work.

title: 73  count()
text:
73.5 Interpret the Result
county_smoker_counts breaks the earlier single smoker count (75 No, 25 Yes) down further by county — for example, Oakford has the highest proportion of smokers relative to its size (6 of 17, roughly 35%), while Cedar Falls has the lowest (3 of 20, 15%).

Common Mistakes

Trying to count() a column that doesn’t exist or is misspelled. Like every dplyr verb, count() uses bare column names — a typo (count(participants, contry)) produces a “column not found” error, not a silent empty result.
Forgetting count() already includes an implicit group_by(). You don’t need to call group_by() first — count(participants, county) handles the grouping and summarising in one step internally.
Confusing count()’s output column name. The count column is always named n by default (not count or freq) — referencing county_smoker_counts$count will fail; it must be county_smoker_counts$n.

title: 73  count()
text:
73.6 Practice Problem
Use count() to find how many participants fall into each combination of sex and smoker. Which combination is least common?

title: 73  count()
text:
73.7 Challenge Problem
Use count(participants, county, sort = TRUE) to confirm Springvale is the largest county, then use count(participants, county, smoker) to determine whether Springvale also has the largest number of smokers (not necessarily the same as the largest proportion).

title: 73  count()
text:
73.8 Key Takeaways

count(df, column) is a one-line shortcut for group_by() + summarise(n = n()), with the result column always named n.
count() can take multiple columns to count every combination that actually occurs in the data.
sort = TRUE reorders the result largest-count-first, useful for quickly spotting the most (or least) common categories.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What is count(df, column) equivalent to?

filter(df, column)
group_by(df, column) + summarise(n = n())
arrange(df, column)
select(df, column)

True or false. The count column produced by count() is always named n.
Predict the output. What would count(participants, smoker) return, given 75 “No” and 25 “Yes” values?
Write the code. Write the count() call to count participants per combination of county and sex, sorted largest-first.



Show answers


b) group_by(df, column) + summarise(n = n()).
True. count()’s result column is always named n by default.
A two-row data frame: smoker = "No", n = 75 and smoker = "Yes", n = 25.
count(participants, county, sex, sort = TRUE)

title: 74  distinct()
text:
74.1 The R Concept
distinct() returns the unique rows (or unique combinations of specified columns), with no count attached:
This is the tidyverse counterpart to Level 3’s unique(participants$county) — but because distinct() operates on a data frame rather than a single vector, it can also find unique combinations across several columns at once:

title: 74  distinct()
text:
distinct(df, column)
distinct(participants, county)

distinct(participants, county, sex)   # every county-sex combination that occurs

title: 74  distinct()
text:
74.2 Demonstration
library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

distinct(participants, county)
       county
1     Oakford
2   Riverdale
3   Millbrook
4  Springvale
5 Cedar Falls
Five distinct counties — the rows appear in the order they were first encountered in the dataset (row 1 is Oakford because participant #1 lives there), not alphabetically.
nrow(distinct(participants, county))
[1] 5

title: 74  distinct()
text:
74.3 Explore
Try distinct(participants, county, sex) and check nrow() on the result. With 5 counties and 2 sex categories, there are at most 10 possible combinations — confirm whether all 10 actually occur in the data, or whether some combination is missing entirely.

title: 74  distinct()
text:
74.4 Solve the Problem
library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

unique_counties <- distinct(participants, county)

nrow(unique_counties)
[1] 5
unique_county_sex <- distinct(participants, county, sex)

nrow(unique_county_sex)
[1] 10
Five distinct counties confirmed, matching what unique(participants$county) reported in Level 3 and what count(participants, county) implied by returning 5 rows on the previous page. All 10 possible county-and-sex combinations occur at least once in the data.

title: 74  distinct()
text:
74.5 Interpret the Result
distinct() confirms there are exactly 5 unique counties in the study — no unexpected typos or extra category hiding in the data (recall Level 3’s messy dataset, where inconsistent capitalization made “13 distinct-looking values” collapse to only 5 real counties after cleaning). This clean dataset’s county column has no such problem.

Common Mistakes

Confusing distinct() with count(). distinct() returns only the unique values themselves, with no counts attached; count() returns the unique values plus how many rows had each. Reach for distinct() when you only care “what values exist,” and count() when you also care “how many of each.”
Expecting distinct() to sort its output. Unlike arrange(), distinct() returns unique values in the order they first appear in the data, not alphabetically or numerically — combine with arrange() afterward if sorted output matters.
Forgetting distinct() on a whole data frame (no columns specified) checks every column. distinct(participants) with no column names looks for entire duplicate rows across all columns — a different (and much rarer) kind of duplicate than a single repeated county value.

title: 74  distinct()
text:
74.6 Practice Problem
Use distinct() to find every unique combination of smoker and sex that occurs in the dataset, and report how many there are.

title: 74  distinct()
text:
74.7 Challenge Problem
Compare nrow(distinct(participants, county, smoker)) to the number of rows produced by last page’s count(participants, county, smoker). Should these two numbers be the same? Explain why or why not.

title: 74  distinct()
text:
74.8 Key Takeaways

distinct(df, column) returns the unique values of a column (or unique combinations of several columns), with no counts attached.
distinct() is the tidyverse counterpart to Level 3’s unique(), extended to work across multiple columns at once.
Rows appear in first-encountered order, not sorted — pair with arrange() if sorted output is needed.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. How does distinct() differ from count()?

distinct() returns unique values with counts; count() doesn’t
distinct() returns unique values with no counts; count() returns unique values with counts
They are identical in every way
distinct() only works on numeric columns

True or false. distinct(participants, county) sorts the counties alphabetically.
Predict the output. If smoker has only two possible values (“Yes” and “No”), how many rows would distinct(participants, smoker) return?
Write the code. Write the code to find every unique combination of county and smoker in participants.



Show answers


b) distinct() returns unique values with no counts; count() returns unique values with counts.
False. distinct() preserves first-encountered order, not alphabetical order.
2 rows — one for "Yes", one for "No".
distinct(participants, county, smoker)

title: 75  rename()
text:
75.1 The R Concept
rename() renames columns with a direct new_name = old_name mapping — no names() indirection required:
Read the argument order carefully: it’s new name first, old name second — the reverse of how you might instinctively say it aloud (“rename systolic_bp to sbp”), but consistent with every other tidyverse verb’s new_column = <expression> pattern (mutate(), summarise()).

title: 75  rename()
text:
rename(df, new_name = old_name)
rename(participants, sbp = systolic_bp, dbp = diastolic_bp)

title: 75  rename()
text:
75.2 Demonstration
library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

renamed <- rename(participants, sbp = systolic_bp, dbp = diastolic_bp)

names(renamed)
[1] "id"      "age"     "sex"     "county"  "sbp"     "dbp"     "bmi"
[8] "smoker"
systolic_bp and diastolic_bp are gone from the column names; sbp and dbp have taken their place, in the same column positions.

title: 75  rename()
text:
75.3 Explore
Try rename(participants, participant_id = id) and confirm with names() that only id changed, while every other column name — including its position in the data frame — stayed exactly the same. rename() never reorders columns, unlike select().

title: 75  rename()
text:
75.4 Solve the Problem
library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

for_collaborator <- rename(participants,
  sbp = systolic_bp,
  dbp = diastolic_bp
)

names(for_collaborator)
[1] "id"      "age"     "sex"     "county"  "sbp"     "dbp"     "bmi"
[8] "smoker"
head(for_collaborator[, c("sbp", "dbp")], 3)
  sbp dbp
1 125  79
2 123  80
3 130  77
The dataset is ready to share with your professor’s collaborator, using the clearer sbp/dbp names — with every value untouched, only the column labels changed.

title: 75  rename()
text:
75.5 Interpret the Result
for_collaborator has identical data to participants, just with two columns relabeled. This is purely cosmetic — no values changed, no rows were reordered, and nrow()/ncol() are identical to the original.

Common Mistakes

Reversing the argument order. rename(participants, systolic_bp = sbp) would try to rename a column called sbp (which doesn’t exist yet) to systolic_bp — the opposite of the intended renaming. Always read it as “new = old.”
Expecting rename() to reorder columns. Unlike select(), rename() never changes column order or drops any columns — it only relabels the ones you specify, leaving everything else (including position) untouched.
Forgetting to reassign the result. Like every other dplyr verb covered so far, rename() returns a new data frame; participants itself is unaffected unless you write participants <- rename(participants, ...).

title: 75  rename()
text:
75.6 Practice Problem
Use rename() to rename bmi to body_mass_index and county to region, and confirm with names() that both changes took effect while all other column names and their order stayed the same.

title: 75  rename()
text:
75.7 Challenge Problem
Compare Level 4’s base R approach (names(df)[names(df) == "old"] <- "new") against rename() for renaming three columns at once. Which approach scales better as the number of renamed columns grows, and why?

title: 75  rename()
text:
75.8 Key Takeaways

rename(df, new_name = old_name) relabels a column directly — argument order is new name first, old name second.
rename() never reorders or drops columns; it only changes labels, leaving positions and all other columns untouched.
Like other dplyr verbs, rename() returns a new data frame and must be reassigned to keep the change.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. In rename(df, new_name = old_name), which comes first?

The old name
The new name
Order doesn’t matter
Neither — both must be quoted strings in a vector

True or false. rename() can change the order of columns in a data frame.
Predict the output. After df2 <- rename(df, patient_id = id), does df still have a column called id?
Find the error.
rename(participants, systolic_bp = sbp)
# intended to rename systolic_bp to sbp



Show answers


b) The new name comes first, then =, then the old name.
False. rename() only changes column labels — it never reorders or drops columns.
Yes — rename() returns a new data frame (df2); the original df is completely unaffected.
The argument order is reversed. Since participants has a column named systolic_bp (not sbp), this call tries to rename a nonexistent sbp column. It should be rename(participants, sbp = systolic_bp).

title: 76  across()
text:
76.1 The R Concept
across() applies one function to several columns inside summarise() or mutate(), instead of writing one summary expression per column:
The ~ starts a short formula-style function, where .x stands in for “whichever column across() is currently processing” — the first time through, .x is age; the second time, .x is systolic_bp; and so on. This is the same “apply one operation across many things” idea as Level 10’s loops, just expressed as a single dplyr call rather than an explicit for loop.

title: 76  across()
text:
summarise(df, across(c(col1, col2, col3), ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)))
summarise(participants, across(c(age, systolic_bp, diastolic_bp, bmi), ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)))

title: 76  across()
text:
76.2 Demonstration
library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

summarise(participants,
  across(c(age, systolic_bp, diastolic_bp, bmi), ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE))
)
       age systolic_bp diastolic_bp      bmi
1 47.42553    129.84375        80.44 27.12316
Four means, computed with a single across() expression — each result column keeps the original column’s name, since no renaming was requested.

title: 76  across()
text:
76.3 Explore
Try naming the results by wrapping across()’s function argument in a named list: across(c(age, bmi), list(avg = ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE))). Notice the output columns become age_avg and bmi_avg — across() automatically builds column names by combining the original column name with the function’s label.

title: 76  across()
text:
76.4 Solve the Problem
library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

numeric_summary <- summarise(participants,
  across(c(age, systolic_bp, diastolic_bp, bmi), ~ round(mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE), 2))
)

numeric_summary
    age systolic_bp diastolic_bp   bmi
1 47.43      129.84        80.44 27.12
All four means computed and rounded in one across() call — the same result you’d get from four separate mean(..., na.rm = TRUE) expressions, but without repeating the pattern four times.

title: 76  across()
text:
76.5 Interpret the Result
The four numeric variables average out to age 47.43, systolic BP 129.84, diastolic BP 80.44, and BMI 27.12 — matching exactly the individual means computed on earlier pages, now produced by one concise instruction instead of four nearly-identical ones.

Common Mistakes

Forgetting the ~ before the function. across(cols, mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)) tries to evaluate mean(.x, ...) immediately (before .x has any meaning) and errors; the ~ is what tells R “treat this as a function to apply later, once per column.”
Using . instead of .x. Older tidyverse code sometimes used a single dot (.) as the placeholder; modern across() uses .x — mixing the two conventions is a common source of confusion when reading examples from different tutorial vintages.
Applying across() to non-numeric columns by accident. across(everything(), ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)) would try to compute a mean of county (text) and fail — always specify which columns are actually numeric, or use a selector like where(is.numeric).

title: 76  across()
text:
76.6 Practice Problem
Use across() inside summarise() to compute the maximum value (not mean) of age, systolic_bp, and bmi in a single call.

title: 76  across()
text:
76.7 Challenge Problem
Use across() combined with group_by(participants, county) to compute the mean age and mean bmi for each county in a single summarise(across(...)) call, rather than writing mean_age = ... and mean_bmi = ... as two separate lines.

title: 76  across()
text:
76.8 Key Takeaways

across(c(col1, col2, ...), ~ <function>(.x, ...)) applies one function to several columns inside summarise() or mutate().
The ~ marks a formula-style function, where .x stands in for whichever column is currently being processed.
across() eliminates copy-pasting the same summary expression once per column, the same repetition-avoidance principle behind Level 9’s functions.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What does .x represent inside across(cols, ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE))?

The entire data frame
Whichever column across() is currently processing
The number of columns in cols
A fixed column named x

True or false. across() can only be used with mean(), never other summary functions.
Predict the output. How many result columns would across(c(age, bmi, systolic_bp), ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)) produce inside summarise()?
Find the error.
summarise(participants, across(c(age, bmi), mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)))



Show answers


b) Whichever column across() is currently processing.
False. across() works with any summary function — sum(), max(), sd(), or a custom function.
3 result columns — one for each of age, bmi, and systolic_bp.
The function is missing its leading ~, so R tries to evaluate mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE) immediately, before .x has meaning. It should be across(c(age, bmi), ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)).

title: 77  The Native Pipe |>
text:
77.1 The R Concept
The native pipe, |> (built into base R since version 4.1, no package required), takes whatever is on its left and inserts it as the first argument of the function on its right:
Chained together, a pipe reads top-to-bottom in the exact order the steps happen — no more inside-out nesting:
This is identical to head(filter(participants, age >= 65), 3) — same result, same functions, same arguments — but now written in the order you’d describe it aloud: “take participants, filter to age 65+, then take the first 3.”

title: 77  The Native Pipe |>
text:
x |> f()
# is exactly equivalent to:
f(x)
participants |> filter(age >= 65)
# is exactly equivalent to:
filter(participants, age >= 65)

participants |> filter(age >= 65) |> head(3)

title: 77  The Native Pipe |>
text:
77.2 Demonstration
library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

# Nested version - reads inside-out
nested_result <- head(filter(participants, age >= 65), 3)

# Piped version - reads top-to-bottom
piped_result <- participants |> filter(age >= 65) |> head(3)

identical(nested_result, piped_result)
[1] TRUE
piped_result
  id age    sex      county systolic_bp diastolic_bp  bmi smoker
1 17  79 Female  Springvale         117           73 21.1    Yes
2 19  80 Female Cedar Falls         109           79 28.4     No
3 31  70   Male     Oakford         145           85   NA     No

title: 77  The Native Pipe |>
text:
77.3 Explore
Try adding a third step to the pipeline: participants |> filter(age >= 65) |> arrange(desc(systolic_bp)) |> head(3). Read it aloud as you look at it — “take participants, filter to seniors, sort by blood pressure descending, take the top 3” — and notice the code reads in that exact same order.

title: 77  The Native Pipe |>
text:
77.4 Solve the Problem
library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

at_risk_seniors <- participants |>
  filter(age >= 65 & systolic_bp >= 130) |>
  arrange(desc(systolic_bp))

at_risk_seniors
  id age    sex      county systolic_bp diastolic_bp  bmi smoker
1 62  81   Male   Millbrook         153           95 30.3    Yes
2 31  70   Male     Oakford         145           85   NA     No
3 84  67 Female   Riverdale         134           79 26.8     No
4 51  66   Male Cedar Falls         133           77 34.5     No
Filtering and sorting are now two clearly ordered steps in one pipeline — read top to bottom, this code says exactly what it does: “take participants, filter to at-risk seniors, sort by blood pressure with the highest first.”

title: 77  The Native Pipe |>
text:
77.5 Interpret the Result
The same 4 at-risk seniors identified back on the filter() page are now sorted by systolic blood pressure, highest first — participant #62 (age 81, BP 153) stands out as the most urgent case, followed by #31, #84, and #51. The pipe made the two-step process (“filter, then sort”) visibly sequential rather than nested inside one another.

Common Mistakes

Confusing the native pipe |> with the older magrittr pipe %>%. You may see %>% in older tidyverse tutorials and Stack Overflow answers — it behaves almost identically, but |> is built into base R itself (no library() call needed) and is now the standard choice for new code.
Forgetting the pipe inserts as the first argument only. participants |> filter(age >= 65) becomes filter(participants, age >= 65) — participants always lands in the first argument slot. If a function’s data argument isn’t first, the plain pipe can’t simply slot it in.
Piping into something that isn’t a function call. participants |> age is an error — the right-hand side of |> must be a function call like filter(...) or head(...), not a bare column reference.

title: 77  The Native Pipe |>
text:
77.6 Practice Problem
Rewrite arrange(select(participants, id, age, bmi), desc(bmi)) as a piped chain using |>, and confirm with identical() that both versions produce the same result.

title: 77  The Native Pipe |>
text:
77.7 Challenge Problem
Write a three-step pipeline that starts from participants, filters to non-smokers (smoker == "No"), sorts by age ascending, and keeps only the id, age, and county columns — using |> to chain filter(), arrange(), and select() in that order.

title: 77  The Native Pipe |>
text:
77.8 Key Takeaways

x |> f() is exactly equivalent to f(x) — the pipe inserts its left-hand side as the function’s first argument.
Chained pipes read top-to-bottom, in the actual order operations happen — the opposite of nested function calls, which read inside-out.
The native pipe |> is built into base R (since R 4.1) and requires no package; you may also encounter the older, package-based %>% pipe in existing code.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What is participants |> filter(age >= 65) equivalent to?

filter(age >= 65, participants)
filter(participants, age >= 65)
age >= 65 |> filter(participants)
None of these — the pipe changes the result

True or false. The native pipe |> requires library(magrittr) or library(dplyr) to work.
Predict the output. What would participants |> nrow() return?
Find the error.
participants |> age



Show answers


b) filter(participants, age >= 65) — the pipe’s left side becomes the first argument.
False. The native pipe |> is built into base R (since version 4.1) and needs no package.
100 — the number of rows in participants, since nrow() takes a data frame and participants is piped in as that argument.
The right-hand side of |> must be a function call, not a bare column reference. age alone isn’t a function call, so this errors; you’d need something like participants |> pull(age) (or simply participants$age) instead.

title: 78  Chaining Operations
text:
78.1 The R Concept
A full analytical pipeline chains every relevant verb together, each one passing its result to the next:
Each step in this chain does exactly one job — filter narrows the rows, group_by() sets up per-group processing, summarise() collapses each group to one row, and arrange() orders the final result — and the pipe (|>) makes the whole sequence read top-to-bottom, in the order the steps actually happen.

title: 78  Chaining Operations
78  Chaining Operations
text:
78.2 Demonstration
library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

older_participants_summary <- participants |>
  filter(age >= 50) |>
  group_by(county) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_bp = round(mean(systolic_bp, na.rm = TRUE), 2)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(mean_bp))

older_participants_summary
# A tibble: 5 x 3
  county          n mean_bp
  <chr>       <int>   <dbl>
1 Millbrook       9  130.38
2 Oakford         3  130.00
3 Springvale     13  125.77
4 Cedar Falls    11  125.40
5 Riverdale       9  122.00
One pipeline, four chained steps, answering the exact question asked — no intermediate objects needed along the way.

title: 78  Chaining Operations
text:
78.3 Explore
Try removing the final arrange(desc(mean_bp)) step and re-running the pipeline. Notice the same five rows appear, just in whatever order group_by() happened to encounter the counties — confirming that arrange() only affects display order, not which rows or values are present.

title: 78  Chaining Operations
text:
78.4 Solve the Problem
library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

bp_by_county_50plus <- participants |>
  filter(age >= 50) |>
  group_by(county) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_bp = round(mean(systolic_bp, na.rm = TRUE), 2)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(mean_bp))

bp_by_county_50plus
# A tibble: 5 x 3
  county          n mean_bp
  <chr>       <int>   <dbl>
1 Millbrook       9  130.38
2 Oakford         3  130.00
3 Springvale     13  125.77
4 Cedar Falls    11  125.40
5 Riverdale       9  122.00
Among participants aged 50 and older, Millbrook has the highest average systolic blood pressure (130.38, across 9 participants), narrowly ahead of Oakford (130.00, but only 3 participants). Riverdale has the lowest (122.00) — your professor’s exact question, answered in one pipeline.

title: 78  Chaining Operations
text:
78.5 Interpret the Result
This single chained pipeline — filter, group, summarise, arrange — replaced what would otherwise be four or five separate base R steps with intermediate objects at each stage. Notice too that Oakford’s second-place mean (130.00) rests on only 3 participants, a much shakier estimate than Millbrook’s 9 — a reminder from Level 11 (statistics) that group size matters when interpreting a mean, not just the mean itself.

Common Mistakes

Grouping before filtering. group_by() should come after filter() in most pipelines — you generally want to narrow the rows first, then split what remains into groups, not the reverse.
Forgetting arrange() only changes the final display order. Placing arrange() in the middle of a pipeline (before summarise(), for instance) sorts rows that will just get collapsed a step later, having no effect on the final grouped result — arrange() belongs at the very end unless you have a specific reason otherwise.
Overlooking small group sizes when interpreting grouped summaries. A mean based on 3 participants (Oakford) and a mean based on 9 (Millbrook) aren’t equally trustworthy, even though summarise() reports them with the same apparent precision.

title: 78  Chaining Operations
text:
78.6 Practice Problem
Write a chained pipeline that starts from participants, filters to current smokers (smoker == "Yes"), groups by county, computes the mean bmi and count per county, and sorts by mean bmi descending. Which county has the highest mean BMI among smokers?

title: 78  Chaining Operations
text:
78.7 Challenge Problem
Write a pipeline that filters to participants with bmi >= 30 (obese, from Level 4’s case_when() categories), groups by county and smoker, computes the count for each county-and-smoking-status combination, and sorts by count descending. Which single county-and-smoking-status combination has the most obese participants?

title: 78  Chaining Operations
text:
78.8 Key Takeaways

A full pipeline chains filter() → group_by() → summarise() → arrange() (in that order, generally) using |>, replacing multiple intermediate base R objects with one readable block.
arrange() belongs at the end of a pipeline — it only affects display order and has no effect if placed before a summarise() that will collapse the rows anyway.
Small group sizes can produce misleadingly precise-looking summary statistics — always check n alongside any grouped mean before drawing conclusions.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. In a pipeline df |> filter(...) |> group_by(...) |> summarise(...) |> arrange(...), what does arrange() do?

Filters rows before grouping
Changes the final display order of the summarised result
Groups the data a second time
Removes duplicate rows

True or false. Placing arrange() in the middle of a pipeline, right before summarise(), changes the final grouped-and-summarised result.
Predict the output. In bp_by_county_50plus, which county has the second-highest mean blood pressure, and how many participants does that count rely on?
Find the error.
participants |>
  group_by(county) |>
  filter(age >= 50) |>
  summarise(mean_bp = mean(systolic_bp, na.rm = TRUE))



Show answers


b) Changes the final display order of the summarised result.
False. arrange() before summarise() sorts rows that are about to be collapsed anyway — it has no effect on the final grouped result. Only arrange() placed after summarise() affects the final output’s order.
Oakford, with mean blood pressure 130.00 — but that mean rests on only 3 participants, far fewer than Millbrook’s 9.
This isn’t strictly an error — group_by() before filter() still works, since filter() operates fine on grouped data — but it’s misleading style: readers expect a pipeline to narrow rows before grouping them, and grouping first can silently change behavior in more complex pipelines involving group-specific filters (e.g. filter(age == max(age)), which behaves differently before vs. after grouping).

title: Level 6 — Data Reshaping
text:
What you’ll build toward
You’ll start with a repeated-measures dataset — blood pressure and weight recorded across three clinic visits for 28 participants, arriving one row per participant with a column per visit (“wide” format). You’ll reshape it to one row per participant-visit combination (“long” format) for tidy analysis, split and combine compound columns, and reshape multiple measurements at once. Then you’ll shift to a second, equally common real-world problem: combining two separate files — a baseline enrollment file and a one-year follow-up file — using every major type of dplyr join, and finish with a capstone that reshapes and joins in a single pipeline.

title: Level 6 — Data Reshaping
text:
Topics in this level

Wide format vs. long format — what each looks like and when each is useful
pivot_longer() and pivot_wider()
separate() and unite() — splitting and combining columns
Reshaping multiple measurements at once with .value
Keys, and one-to-one vs. one-to-many relationships
left_join(), inner_join(), right_join(), full_join()
semi_join() and anti_join()
A combined reshaping-and-joining capstone

title: Level 6 — Data Reshaping
text:
A preview mission
Your mission across this level: reshape a three-visit blood pressure dataset to compute how much each participant’s blood pressure changed over time, then merge a baseline enrollment file with a follow-up visit file — collected a year apart, by different teams — into one analysis-ready dataset without losing or duplicating a single participant.

title: 79  Wide Format vs. Long Format
text:
79.1 The R Concept
Wide format stores one row per observational unit (here, one row per participant), with separate columns for each repeated measurement:
Long format stores one row per combination of observational unit and measurement occasion, with the measurement occasion itself living in a column:
Both represent the exact same information — no data is gained or lost — but long format has an actual visit column you can filter(), group_by(), or plot against, while wide format encodes that same information into column names that dplyr functions can’t directly reference as values.
crumbs: 79  Wide Format vs. Long Format

title: 79  Wide Format vs. Long Format
text:
id  sbp_visit1  sbp_visit2  sbp_visit3
1   125         120         117
2   123         121         129

id  visit   sbp
1   visit1  125
1   visit2  120
1   visit3  117
2   visit1  123
2   visit2  121
2   visit3  129
crumbs: 79  Wide Format vs. Long Format

title: 79  Wide Format vs. Long Format
text:
79.2 Demonstration
bp_wide <- read.csv("data/bp_visits_wide.csv")

names(bp_wide)
[1] "id"           "county"       "clinic_site"  "sbp_visit1"
[5] "sbp_visit2"   "sbp_visit3"   "weight_visit1" "weight_visit2"
[9] "weight_visit3"
nrow(bp_wide)
[1] 28
28 rows — one per participant — with visit information spread across six separate measurement columns (three for sbp, three for weight).
crumbs: 79  Wide Format vs. Long Format

title: 79  Wide Format vs. Long Format
text:
79.3 Explore
Try mean(bp_wide$sbp_visit1, na.rm = TRUE), then mean(bp_wide$sbp_visit2, na.rm = TRUE), then mean(bp_wide$sbp_visit3, na.rm = TRUE) — three separate lines, one per visit, because there’s no single sbp column to group_by(visit) on. This repetition is exactly the signal that a reshape is needed.
crumbs: 79  Wide Format vs. Long Format

title: 79  Wide Format vs. Long Format
text:
79.4 Solve the Problem
bp_wide <- read.csv("data/bp_visits_wide.csv")

# Wide format: three repeated columns, one row per participant
head(bp_wide[, c("id", "sbp_visit1", "sbp_visit2", "sbp_visit3")], 3)
  id sbp_visit1 sbp_visit2 sbp_visit3
1  1        125        120        117
2  2        123        121        129
3  3        130        128        124
There is no way to group_by() a “visit” here, because “visit” isn’t a column of values — it’s spread across three column names. The next page’s pivot_longer() is exactly the tool that fixes this.
crumbs: 79  Wide Format vs. Long Format

title: 79  Wide Format vs. Long Format
text:
79.5 Interpret the Result
bp_wide is a perfectly valid, common way to store repeated-measures data — and it’s often the easier format for a human to read at a glance, comparing one participant’s three visits side by side. But it’s the wrong shape for group_by(visit)-style analysis or for plotting change over time, which is why reshaping tools exist at all: not to “fix” wide format, but to convert between the shape that’s easy to read and the shape that’s easy to analyze.

Common Mistakes

Assuming one format is objectively “correct.” Neither wide nor long is inherently better — wide is often better for reading and data entry; long is often better for group_by()-style analysis and plotting. The right shape depends on the task.
Trying to group_by() a value that’s actually a column name. group_by(bp_wide, visit) fails outright — there is no visit column in wide format; the visit information lives in the column names sbp_visit1/sbp_visit2/sbp_visit3, not in any column’s values.
Forgetting reshaping doesn’t change the underlying data. Converting wide to long (or back) doesn’t add, remove, or recalculate anything — it only changes which pieces of information are stored as column names versus column values.
crumbs: 79  Wide Format vs. Long Format

title: 79  Wide Format vs. Long Format
text:
79.6 Practice Problem
Look at bp_wide’s weight_visit1, weight_visit2, weight_visit3 columns. Without writing any reshaping code yet, describe in words what the long-format equivalent would look like: what would the column names be, and how many rows would it have?
crumbs: 79  Wide Format vs. Long Format

title: 79  Wide Format vs. Long Format
text:
79.7 Challenge Problem
If bp_wide has 28 rows and 3 visits’ worth of sbp columns, how many rows would a long-format version (one row per participant-visit combination, sbp only) have? What if you also included the weight measurements in the same long format?
crumbs: 79  Wide Format vs. Long Format

title: 79  Wide Format vs. Long Format
text:
79.8 Key Takeaways

Wide format has one row per unit, with repeated measurements spread across multiple columns.
Long format has one row per unit-and-occasion combination, with the occasion itself stored as an actual column of values.
Both formats hold identical information — the right choice depends on whether you’re reading data by eye or feeding it into group_by()-style analysis or plotting.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. In wide format, how is repeated measurement information (like “visit number”) typically stored?

In an actual column of values
Encoded in the column names
It cannot be stored in wide format at all
As row names

True or false. Converting data from wide to long format changes the underlying values.
Predict the output. If bp_wide has 28 rows and 3 visit columns for sbp, how many rows would the fully reshaped long format (sbp only) have?
Short answer. Why can’t you group_by(bp_wide, visit) directly on the wide-format data?



Show answers


b) Encoded in the column names (e.g., sbp_visit1, sbp_visit2).
False. Reshaping only changes the shape of the data (which information is a column name vs. a column value) — no values are added, removed, or recalculated.
84 rows (28 participants × 3 visits).
There is no visit column in the wide-format data — the visit information is embedded in the column names (sbp_visit1, etc.), not stored as values group_by() can reference.
crumbs: 79  Wide Format vs. Long Format

title: 80  pivot_longer()
text:
80.1 The R Concept
pivot_longer(), from the tidyr package (part of the tidyverse, alongside dplyr), takes multiple columns and stacks them into two new columns: one holding the former column names, one holding the former column values:
cols accepts the same starts_with() / ends_with() / contains() helpers from Level 5’s select() — genuinely the same tidyselect syntax, reused here for a different purpose. Adding names_prefix = "sbp_" strips a shared prefix from the resulting names column, turning "sbp_visit1" into the cleaner "visit1".

title: 80  pivot_longer()
text:
pivot_longer(df,
  cols = c(col1, col2, col3),   # or a tidyselect helper like starts_with()
  names_to = "name_column",
  values_to = "value_column"
)
pivot_longer(bp_wide,
  cols = starts_with("sbp_visit"),
  names_to = "visit",
  values_to = "sbp"
)

title: 80  pivot_longer()
text:
80.2 Demonstration
library(tidyr)

bp_wide <- read.csv("data/bp_visits_wide.csv")

bp_long_sbp <- pivot_longer(bp_wide,
  cols = starts_with("sbp_visit"),
  names_to = "visit",
  names_prefix = "sbp_",
  values_to = "sbp"
)

nrow(bp_long_sbp)
[1] 84
28 participants × 3 visits = 84 rows, exactly as predicted at the end of the previous page.
head(bp_long_sbp[, c("id", "county", "visit", "sbp")], 6)
  id   county  visit sbp
1  1  Oakford visit1 125
2  1  Oakford visit2 120
3  1  Oakford visit3 117
4  2 Riverdale visit1 123
5  2 Riverdale visit2 121
6  2 Riverdale visit3 129
Participant #1’s three visits are now three separate rows, with an actual visit column you could group_by() — exactly what wide format couldn’t offer.

title: 80  pivot_longer()
text:
80.3 Explore
Try group_by(bp_long_sbp, visit) followed by summarise(mean_sbp = mean(sbp, na.rm = TRUE)) (Level 5). Confirm you can now compute a mean blood pressure per visit — something that was impossible without first reshaping.

title: 80  pivot_longer()
text:
80.4 Solve the Problem
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

bp_wide <- read.csv("data/bp_visits_wide.csv")

bp_long_sbp <- pivot_longer(bp_wide,
  cols = starts_with("sbp_visit"),
  names_to = "visit",
  names_prefix = "sbp_",
  values_to = "sbp"
)

visit_means <- bp_long_sbp |>
  group_by(visit) |>
  summarise(mean_sbp = round(mean(sbp, na.rm = TRUE), 2))

visit_means
# A tibble: 3 x 2
  visit  mean_sbp
  <chr>     <dbl>
1 visit1   124.96
2 visit2   120.96
3 visit3   120.69
Now that “visit” is a real column, group_by(visit) works exactly as it did for county in Level 5 — mean systolic blood pressure drops from 124.96 at visit 1 to 120.96 at visit 2, then levels off slightly at 120.69 by visit 3.

title: 80  pivot_longer()
text:
80.5 Interpret the Result
Blood pressure dropped noticeably between visit 1 and visit 2 on average, with a much smaller further change by visit 3 — a pattern impossible to see from the wide-format data without either writing three separate mean() calls or reshaping first. Note also that visit3’s mean is based on fewer participants (26, since 2 dropped out — visible if you check n() per group), a detail worth flagging before drawing conclusions, echoing Level 5’s small-group-size caution.

Common Mistakes

Forgetting cols needs to select only the columns you want stacked. If bp_wide also has weight_visit1/weight_visit2/weight_visit3 columns and you only specify cols = starts_with("sbp_visit"), those weight columns stay wide — and get duplicated across every new long row for a participant, since each of their 3 sbp rows now repeats the same weight values. This isn’t wrong, just worth noticing (the next multiple-value page shows how to pivot both measurements together, avoiding the duplication).
Mixing up names_to and values_to. names_to names the column that will hold the former column names (like "visit1"); values_to names the column that will hold the former column values (like 125). Swapping them produces a working but confusingly labeled result.
Forgetting library(tidyr). pivot_longer() lives in the tidyr package, not dplyr — both are part of the tidyverse, but they’re technically separate packages.

title: 80  pivot_longer()
text:
80.6 Practice Problem
Use pivot_longer() to reshape only the weight_visit1/weight_visit2/weight_visit3 columns into long format, with names_to = "visit" and values_to = "weight". Confirm the result has 84 rows.

title: 80  pivot_longer()
text:
80.7 Challenge Problem
After reshaping sbp to long format, use group_by(visit) and summarise() to compute both the mean and the count (n()) of non-missing sbp values per visit. Which visit has the smallest sample size, and why might that be (hint: recall the dropout pattern from data/bp_visits_wide.csv)?

title: 80  pivot_longer()
text:
80.8 Key Takeaways

pivot_longer(df, cols = ..., names_to = "...", values_to = "...") stacks multiple wide columns into two long columns: one for former names, one for former values.
cols accepts the same tidyselect helpers (starts_with(), etc.) as Level 5’s select().
Reshaping to long format is often the necessary first step before group_by()-based analysis across a repeated measurement like “visit.”


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What does names_to specify in pivot_longer()?

The name of the column holding former column values
The name of the column holding former column names
Which columns to pivot
The final row count

True or false. pivot_longer()’s cols argument can use starts_with() and other tidyselect helpers from Level 5.
Predict the output. If a wide data frame has 10 rows and 4 columns matching a pivot_longer() call’s cols, how many rows will the long-format result have?
Find the error.
pivot_longer(bp_wide, cols = starts_with("sbp_visit"), names_to = "sbp", values_to = "visit")



Show answers


b) The name of the column holding former column names.
True. pivot_longer()’s cols argument uses the same tidyselect syntax as select().
40 rows (10 rows × 4 pivoted columns).
names_to and values_to are swapped — this labels the column names ("sbp_visit1", etc.) as sbp and the blood pressure values (125, etc.) as visit, exactly backwards. It should be names_to = "visit", values_to = "sbp".

title: 81  pivot_wider()
text:
81.1 The R Concept
pivot_wider() takes one column of names and one column of values, and spreads them back out into separate columns:
This is exactly the reverse of pivot_longer()’s names_to/values_to: names_from says which column’s values should become new column names, and values_from says which column’s values should fill in those new columns.

title: 81  pivot_wider()
text:
pivot_wider(df,
  names_from = name_column,
  values_from = value_column
)
pivot_wider(county_visit_means,
  names_from = visit,
  values_from = mean_sbp
)

title: 81  pivot_wider()
text:
81.2 Demonstration
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

bp_wide <- read.csv("data/bp_visits_wide.csv")

bp_long_sbp <- pivot_longer(bp_wide,
  cols = starts_with("sbp_visit"),
  names_to = "visit",
  names_prefix = "sbp_",
  values_to = "sbp"
)

county_visit_means <- bp_long_sbp |>
  group_by(county, visit) |>
  summarise(mean_sbp = round(mean(sbp, na.rm = TRUE), 2), .groups = "drop")

county_visit_means
# A tibble: 15 x 3
   county      visit  mean_sbp
   <chr>       <chr>     <dbl>
 1 Cedar Falls visit1   136.00
 2 Cedar Falls visit2   130.25
 3 Cedar Falls visit3   128.00
 4 Millbrook   visit1   122.17
 5 Millbrook   visit2   120.33
 6 Millbrook   visit3   122.00
 7 Oakford     visit1   120.67
 8 Oakford     visit2   117.17
 9 Oakford     visit3   119.50
10 Riverdale   visit1   123.33
11 Riverdale   visit2   119.00
12 Riverdale   visit3   120.67
13 Springvale  visit1   126.33
14 Springvale  visit2   121.17
15 Springvale  visit3   116.17
15 rows, exactly as group_by(county, visit) would produce (5 counties × 3 visits) — readable, but each county’s three visits are spread across three separate rows.
report_table <- pivot_wider(county_visit_means,
  names_from = visit,
  values_from = mean_sbp
)

report_table
# A tibble: 5 x 4
  county      visit1 visit2 visit3
  <chr>        <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>
1 Cedar Falls 136.00 130.25 128.00
2 Millbrook   122.17 120.33 122.00
3 Oakford     120.67 117.17 119.50
4 Riverdale   123.33 119.00 120.67
5 Springvale  126.33 121.17 116.17
Now 5 rows (one per county) and 3 visit columns — exactly the compact report table your professor asked for.

title: 81  pivot_wider()
text:
81.3 Explore
Try pivot_wider() on bp_long_sbp directly (not the summarised version), using names_from = visit, values_from = sbp. Confirm this reconstructs something very close to the original bp_wide data frame — pivot_wider() is the true inverse of pivot_longer(), so reshaping long-to-wide and back again returns you to (almost) where you started.

title: 81  pivot_wider()
text:
81.4 Solve the Problem
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

bp_wide <- read.csv("data/bp_visits_wide.csv")

bp_long_sbp <- pivot_longer(bp_wide,
  cols = starts_with("sbp_visit"),
  names_to = "visit",
  names_prefix = "sbp_",
  values_to = "sbp"
)

county_visit_report <- bp_long_sbp |>
  group_by(county, visit) |>
  summarise(mean_sbp = round(mean(sbp, na.rm = TRUE), 2), .groups = "drop") |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = visit, values_from = mean_sbp)

county_visit_report
# A tibble: 5 x 4
  county      visit1 visit2 visit3
  <chr>        <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>
1 Cedar Falls 136.00 130.25 128.00
2 Millbrook   122.17 120.33 122.00
3 Oakford     120.67 117.17 119.50
4 Riverdale   123.33 119.00 120.67
5 Springvale  126.33 121.17 116.17
Cedar Falls starts with the highest mean blood pressure (136.00) and shows the clearest downward trend across visits (136.00 → 130.25 → 128.00), while Springvale shows the largest overall drop from visit 1 to visit 3 (126.33 → 116.17).

title: 81  pivot_wider()
text:
81.5 Interpret the Result
county_visit_report compresses 15 long-format rows into 5 wide-format rows, one per county, with visits laid out side by side — exactly the shape a reader scanning a report wants, even though group_by() + summarise() (Level 5) only knows how to produce the longer, stacked version. Note that Cedar Falls has only 4 participants in this subset (versus 6 in each other county), so its more dramatic-looking trend rests on a notably smaller sample.

Common Mistakes

Forgetting pivot_wider() needs unique name-value combinations. If county_visit_means had two rows with the same county and visit (a duplicate), pivot_wider() would need to combine them into a list-column rather than a single value — a common source of confusing “list of lists” output for beginners.
Confusing names_from and values_from. names_from picks which column’s values become the new column headers; values_from picks which column’s values fill in underneath them. Swapping them produces an error or nonsensical column names.
Forgetting small group sizes remain small after reshaping. Cedar Falls’ n=4 doesn’t become more reliable just because its numbers are now displayed in a wide table instead of a long one — reshaping changes layout, not sample size.

title: 81  pivot_wider()
text:
81.6 Practice Problem
Use group_by(county, visit) and summarise() to compute the mean weight per county per visit (after first pivoting the weight columns to long format), then use pivot_wider() to build a county-by-visit weight report table.

title: 81  pivot_wider()
text:
81.7 Challenge Problem
Starting from bp_long_sbp, use pivot_wider(names_from = visit, values_from = sbp) directly (no group_by()/summarise() first) to reconstruct a wide table. Compare its columns to the original bp_wide’s sbp_visit1/sbp_visit2/sbp_visit3 columns — are the values identical?

title: 81  pivot_wider()
text:
81.8 Key Takeaways

pivot_wider(df, names_from = ..., values_from = ...) is the reverse of pivot_longer(), spreading long rows back into wide columns.
names_from selects which column’s values become new column names; values_from selects which column fills them in.
pivot_wider() requires unique name-value combinations per row group — duplicates produce list-columns instead of single values.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What does names_from specify in pivot_wider()?

Which column’s values become new column names
Which column holds the final row count
Which column to group by
Which column’s values fill in the new columns

True or false. pivot_wider() is the reverse operation of pivot_longer().
Predict the output. If a long-format table has 5 counties and 3 visits (15 rows total), how many rows would pivot_wider(names_from = visit, values_from = mean_sbp) produce?
Find the error.
pivot_wider(county_visit_means, names_from = mean_sbp, values_from = visit)



Show answers


a) Which column’s values become new column names.
True. pivot_wider() reverses what pivot_longer() does, spreading long rows back into wide columns.
5 rows — one per county, since each county’s 3 visit-rows collapse into 1 wide row with 3 visit columns.
names_from and values_from are swapped — this would try to use the numeric mean_sbp values as column names and the text visit values as the data filling them in, which is backwards. It should be names_from = visit, values_from = mean_sbp.

title: 82  separate()
text:
82.1 The R Concept
separate() splits one column into multiple columns, based on a separator character:
into names the new columns, in the order the split pieces appear (left piece first); sep specifies the character (or pattern) to split on — here, a hyphen.

title: 82  separate()
text:
separate(df, col, into = c("new_col1", "new_col2"), sep = "separator")
separate(bp_wide, clinic_site, into = c("county_check", "direction"), sep = "-")

title: 82  separate()
text:
82.2 Demonstration
library(tidyr)

bp_wide <- read.csv("data/bp_visits_wide.csv")

bp_split <- separate(bp_wide, clinic_site,
  into = c("county_check", "direction"),
  sep = "-"
)

head(bp_split[, c("id", "county", "county_check", "direction")], 4)
  id      county county_check direction
1  1     Oakford      Oakford     North
2  2   Riverdale    Riverdale     South
3  3     Oakford      Oakford      East
4  4   Millbrook    Millbrook      West
clinic_site is gone from the result; county_check and direction have taken its place — and notice county_check matches the existing county column exactly, confirming the split worked correctly.

title: 82  separate()
text:
82.3 Explore
Try table(bp_split$direction). With 28 participants across 4 directions (North, South, East, West), confirm the counts are as you’d expect, then try identical(bp_split$county, bp_split$county_check) to formally verify the split matches the original county column for every row.

title: 82  separate()
text:
82.4 Solve the Problem
library(tidyr)

bp_wide <- read.csv("data/bp_visits_wide.csv")

bp_split <- separate(bp_wide, clinic_site,
  into = c("county_check", "direction"),
  sep = "-"
)

table(bp_split$direction)
 East North South  West
    7     7     7     7
identical(bp_split$county, bp_split$county_check)
[1] TRUE
Clinic direction is now its own column, evenly split 7-7-7-7 across North/South/East/West, ready for your professor’s direction-based analysis — and the identical() check confirms the split extracted exactly the county information already present elsewhere, with nothing lost or corrupted.

title: 82  separate()
text:
82.5 Interpret the Result
direction can now be used in group_by(), filter(), or anywhere else a normal column works — something impossible while it was fused into clinic_site as compound text. The county_check column, having served its verification purpose, would typically be dropped (select(-county_check)) once confirmed correct, since keeping a genuine duplicate of county around isn’t useful going forward.

Common Mistakes

Getting into’s column order backwards. into = c("county_check", "direction") assumes the first split piece (before the separator) is county and the second is direction — matching "Oakford-North"’s actual left-to-right order. If the original text were reversed, you’d need to reverse into as well.
Choosing a sep that appears more than once in the data. If a county name itself contained a hyphen, sep = "-" would split it into more than two pieces, breaking the intended two-column split — always check for separator characters appearing unexpectedly inside the values themselves.
Forgetting separate() removes the original column by default. After separate(df, clinic_site, ...), there is no more clinic_site column in the result unless you explicitly keep it (with the remove = FALSE argument).

title: 82  separate()
text:
82.6 Practice Problem
Use separate() to split clinic_site into county_check and direction, then use filter() (Level 5) to find every participant seen at a "North" clinic.

title: 82  separate()
text:
82.7 Challenge Problem
After separating clinic_site, use group_by(direction) and summarise() to compute the mean sbp_visit1 for each clinic direction. Does blood pressure appear to differ meaningfully by clinic direction, or does this look more like random noise given the small group sizes (7 participants per direction)?

title: 82  separate()
text:
82.8 Key Takeaways

separate(df, col, into = c("new1", "new2"), sep = "...") splits one column into multiple columns based on a separator.
into’s column names must be given in the same left-to-right order the split pieces appear in the original values.
separate() removes the original compound column by default, replacing it with the split-out pieces.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. In separate(df, col, into = c("a", "b"), sep = "-"), if col contains "X-Y", what does a become?

"X-Y"
"X"
"Y"
NA

True or false. separate() keeps the original compound column in the result by default.
Predict the output. If clinic_site contains "Millbrook-East", what would direction equal after separating with sep = "-"?
Find the error.
separate(bp_wide, clinic_site, into = c("direction", "county_check"), sep = "-")



Show answers


b) "X" — the first split piece (before the separator) becomes the first into column.
False. separate() removes the original column by default unless remove = FALSE is specified.
"East".
into’s order is backwards — since clinic_site values are formatted as "county-direction" (e.g., "Millbrook-East"), the first piece is the county and the second is the direction. This code would incorrectly label "Millbrook" as direction and "East" as county_check. It should be into = c("county_check", "direction").

title: 83  unite()
text:
83.1 The R Concept
unite() combines multiple columns into one, joined by a separator:
This is exactly the reverse of separate(): instead of naming a sep to split by, you name a sep to join with. The new column name (record_id) comes first, followed by the columns to combine, in the order you want them joined.

title: 83  unite()
text:
unite(df, new_col, col1, col2, sep = "separator")
unite(bp_long_sbp, record_id, id, visit, sep = "_")

title: 83  unite()
text:
83.2 Demonstration
library(tidyr)

bp_long_sbp <- pivot_longer(bp_wide,
  cols = starts_with("sbp_visit"),
  names_to = "visit",
  names_prefix = "sbp_",
  values_to = "sbp"
)

bp_united <- unite(bp_long_sbp, record_id, id, visit, sep = "_")

head(bp_united[, c("record_id", "county", "sbp")], 4)
  record_id   county sbp
1    1_visit1  Oakford 125
2    1_visit2  Oakford 120
3    1_visit3  Oakford 117
4    2_visit1 Riverdale 123
id and visit are gone from the result; record_id has taken their place, combining both pieces of information into one text value.

title: 83  unite()
text:
83.3 Explore
Try length(unique(bp_united$record_id)) and compare it to nrow(bp_united). Since every participant-visit combination is unique, these two numbers should match exactly — confirming record_id works as a genuine unique row identifier.

title: 83  unite()
text:
83.4 Solve the Problem
library(tidyr)

bp_long_sbp <- pivot_longer(bp_wide,
  cols = starts_with("sbp_visit"),
  names_to = "visit",
  names_prefix = "sbp_",
  values_to = "sbp"
)

bp_united <- unite(bp_long_sbp, record_id, id, visit, sep = "_")

length(unique(bp_united$record_id))
[1] 84
nrow(bp_united)
[1] 84
84 unique record_id values for 84 rows — confirming every participant-visit combination produced a genuinely unique identifier, exactly what your professor needs for the database export.

title: 83  unite()
text:
83.5 Interpret the Result
record_id now serves as a single, human-readable unique key ("1_visit1", "1_visit2", …) for each row of the long-format data — a common requirement before exporting to external systems that expect one unique identifier column rather than a combination of several.

Common Mistakes

Forgetting unite() converts everything to text. Even though id started as a number, record_id is a character column after uniting — you can no longer do arithmetic on the id portion without separating it back out first.
Choosing a sep that could reappear inside the combined values. If visit values themselves contained an underscore, later trying to separate() this record_id back apart with sep = "_" could split it into more pieces than intended.
Forgetting unite() removes the original columns by default. Like separate(), unite() drops id and visit from the result unless you specify remove = FALSE to keep them alongside the new combined column.

title: 83  unite()
text:
83.6 Practice Problem
Use unite() to combine county and visit into a single column called county_visit, separated by a dash. How many unique county_visit values result?

title: 83  unite()
text:
83.7 Challenge Problem
Use unite() to create record_id (as shown above) with remove = FALSE, keeping the original id and visit columns alongside the new combined one. Then use separate() to split record_id back apart, and confirm with identical() that you recover the original id and visit values (keeping in mind unite() converts id to text, so you may need as.character() on the original for a fair comparison).

title: 83  unite()
text:
83.8 Key Takeaways

unite(df, new_col, col1, col2, sep = "...") combines multiple columns into one text column, joined by a separator — the reverse of separate().
unite() always produces a character (text) column, even if the original columns were numeric.
Like separate(), unite() removes the original columns by default unless remove = FALSE is specified.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What does unite(df, id_visit, id, visit, sep = "_") produce?

Two new columns, id and visit, split apart
One new column, id_visit, combining id and visit with an underscore
A count of unique id values
A filtered data frame with only matching rows

True or false. unite() preserves the original numeric type of a column if it was numeric before combining.
Predict the output. What would unite(df, key, county, visit, sep = "-") produce for a row where county = "Oakford" and visit = "visit1"?
Find the error.
unite(bp_long_sbp, id, visit, record_id, sep = "_")



Show answers


b) One new column, id_visit, combining id and visit with an underscore.
False. unite() always converts the combined result to a character (text) column, even if the inputs were numeric.
"Oakford-visit1".
The new column name must come first, followed by the columns to combine — here id and visit are existing columns, but record_id (the intended new name) is listed last as if it were a third column to combine, which isn’t what was likely intended. It should be unite(bp_long_sbp, record_id, id, visit, sep = "_").

title: 84  Pivoting Multiple Measurements at Once
text:
84.1 The R Concept
Column names like sbp_visit1 and weight_visit1 encode two pieces of information joined by an underscore: which measurement (sbp or weight), and which visit (visit1). The special .value token in names_to, combined with names_sep, tells pivot_longer() to split each column name into pieces and use one specific piece (wherever .value is placed) as the name of a whole new column, rather than folding it into a single generic value column:
Here, sbp_visit1 splits at the underscore into "sbp" and "visit1". Because .value occupies the first position in names_to, that first piece ("sbp") becomes an actual column name in the result, while the second piece ("visit1") becomes a value in a visit column — giving you sbp and weight as two separate, properly reshaped columns, instead of duplicating one across the other.
crumbs: 84  Pivoting Multiple Measurements at Once

title: 84  Pivoting Multiple Measurements at Once
text:
pivot_longer(df,
  cols = matches("visit"),
  names_to = c(".value", "visit"),
  names_sep = "_"
)
pivot_longer(bp_wide,
  cols = c(starts_with("sbp_visit"), starts_with("weight_visit")),
  names_to = c(".value", "visit"),
  names_sep = "_"
)
crumbs: 84  Pivoting Multiple Measurements at Once

title: 84  Pivoting Multiple Measurements at Once
text:
84.2 Demonstration
library(tidyr)

bp_wide <- read.csv("data/bp_visits_wide.csv")

bp_long_both <- pivot_longer(bp_wide,
  cols = c(starts_with("sbp_visit"), starts_with("weight_visit")),
  names_to = c(".value", "visit"),
  names_sep = "_"
)

names(bp_long_both)
[1] "id"          "county"      "clinic_site" "visit"       "sbp"
[6] "weight"
Both sbp and weight now exist as genuine, separate columns — not one duplicated across the other’s long rows.
head(bp_long_both[, c("id", "visit", "sbp", "weight")], 6)
  id  visit sbp weight
1  1 visit1 125  101.4
2  1 visit2 120  100.8
3  1 visit3 117  101.0
4  2 visit1 123   80.1
5  2 visit2 121   79.6
6  2 visit3 129   79.0
crumbs: 84  Pivoting Multiple Measurements at Once

title: 84  Pivoting Multiple Measurements at Once
text:
84.3 Explore
Try nrow(bp_long_both). It should still be 84 (28 participants × 3 visits) — reshaping two measurements together doesn’t create extra rows, since .value correctly keeps sbp and weight as separate columns within the same row, rather than stacking them into additional rows.
crumbs: 84  Pivoting Multiple Measurements at Once

title: 84  Pivoting Multiple Measurements at Once
text:
84.4 Solve the Problem
library(tidyr)

bp_wide <- read.csv("data/bp_visits_wide.csv")

bp_long_both <- pivot_longer(bp_wide,
  cols = c(starts_with("sbp_visit"), starts_with("weight_visit")),
  names_to = c(".value", "visit"),
  names_sep = "_"
)

nrow(bp_long_both)
[1] 84
sum(!is.na(bp_long_both$sbp))
[1] 82
sum(!is.na(bp_long_both$weight))
[1] 82
84 rows (not 168), with both sbp and weight correctly present as their own columns in every row — exactly the properly reshaped structure your professor asked for, with no duplication of one measurement across the other.
crumbs: 84  Pivoting Multiple Measurements at Once

title: 84  Pivoting Multiple Measurements at Once
text:
84.5 Interpret the Result
bp_long_both is now genuinely “tidy”: one row per participant-visit combination, with each measurement type in its own column. This is the format that would let you group_by(visit) and compute means of both sbp and weight in a single summarise(across(...)) call (Level 5), something the earlier single-measurement reshape couldn’t offer without either duplicating data or reshaping twice.

Common Mistakes

Forgetting to include .value in names_to. Without it, pivot_longer() has no way to know that "sbp" and "weight" should become separate columns rather than both being lumped into one undifferentiated value column.
Choosing the wrong names_sep. sbp_visit1 splits correctly on the first underscore into "sbp" and "visit1" — but if a measurement name itself contained an underscore, the split would produce more pieces than the two names_to expects.
Reshaping each measurement separately when they didn’t need to be. Doing two separate pivot_longer() calls (one for sbp, one for weight) and then trying to merge them back together is far more error-prone than using .value to reshape both in a single step.
crumbs: 84  Pivoting Multiple Measurements at Once

title: 84  Pivoting Multiple Measurements at Once
text:
84.6 Practice Problem
Confirm that bp_long_both’s sbp column, restricted to visit == "visit1", matches bp_wide$sbp_visit1 exactly (hint: use filter() from Level 5, then compare with identical() after matching row order by id).
crumbs: 84  Pivoting Multiple Measurements at Once

title: 84  Pivoting Multiple Measurements at Once
text:
84.7 Challenge Problem
Use bp_long_both, group_by(visit), and summarise(across(c(sbp, weight), ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE))) (Level 5’s across()) to compute mean blood pressure and mean weight for each visit in a single call.
crumbs: 84  Pivoting Multiple Measurements at Once

title: 84  Pivoting Multiple Measurements at Once
text:
84.8 Key Takeaways

The .value token in pivot_longer()’s names_to lets you reshape multiple measurement types into separate columns at once, rather than lumping them all into a single generic value column.
names_sep (or names_pattern) tells pivot_longer() how to split each compound column name into its .value and other pieces.
Reshaping multiple measurements together in one call avoids both row-count duplication and the awkwardness of merging separately reshaped pieces back together.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What does the .value token do inside pivot_longer()’s names_to?

Deletes that piece of the column name
Uses that piece of the split column name as an actual new column name
Converts that piece to numeric
Has no special meaning; it’s just a placeholder string

True or false. Reshaping sbp and weight together with .value produces more rows than reshaping sbp alone.
Predict the output. After the .value-based reshape, how many columns would bp_long_both have among id, county, clinic_site, visit, sbp, weight?
Find the error.
pivot_longer(bp_wide,
  cols = c(starts_with("sbp_visit"), starts_with("weight_visit")),
  names_to = "visit",
  names_sep = "_"
)



Show answers


b) Uses that piece of the split column name as an actual new column name.
False. Both reshapes produce 84 rows — .value keeps sbp and weight as separate columns within the same rows, rather than adding extra rows.
6 columns: id, county, clinic_site, visit, sbp, weight.
names_to is missing the .value token — with just "visit", pivot_longer() expects only one piece per column name and can’t know that "sbp" and "weight" should become separate columns. It should be names_to = c(".value", "visit").
crumbs: 84  Pivoting Multiple Measurements at Once

title: 85  Reshaping Mini-Capstone: Change Across Visits
text:
85.1 The R Concept
A reshaping pipeline often goes wide → long → (filter/analyze) → wide again: long format makes it easy to filter() down to exactly the visits you want, and pivoting back to wide afterward aligns the remaining visits side by side so you can compute a per-row difference with mutate():
crumbs: 85  Reshaping Mini-Capstone: Change Across Visits

title: 85  Reshaping Mini-Capstone: Change Across Visits
crumbs: 85  Reshaping Mini-Capstone: Change Across Visits

title: 85  Reshaping Mini-Capstone: Change Across Visits
text:
85.2 Demonstration
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

bp_wide <- read.csv("data/bp_visits_wide.csv")

bp_change <- bp_wide |>
  pivot_longer(cols = starts_with("sbp_visit"), names_to = "visit", names_prefix = "sbp_", values_to = "sbp") |>
  filter(visit %in% c("visit1", "visit3")) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = visit, values_from = sbp) |>
  filter(!is.na(visit3)) |>
  mutate(change = visit3 - visit1)

head(bp_change[, c("id", "county", "visit1", "visit3", "change")], 5)
  id      county visit1 visit3 change
1  1     Oakford    125    117     -8
2  2   Riverdale    123    129      6
3  3     Oakford    130    124     -6
4  4   Millbrook    117    116     -1
5  5   Millbrook    139    138     -1
Filtering out the 2 participants missing a visit3 reading before computing change (via filter(!is.na(visit3))) avoids NA change values contaminating the county averages next.
crumbs: 85  Reshaping Mini-Capstone: Change Across Visits

title: 85  Reshaping Mini-Capstone: Change Across Visits
text:
85.3 Explore
Try skipping the filter(visit %in% c("visit1", "visit3")) step and pivoting all three visits wide instead. Confirm you’d then need mutate(change = visit3 - visit1) regardless — visit2 would just sit unused in the result, since mutate() only references the columns you actually name.
crumbs: 85  Reshaping Mini-Capstone: Change Across Visits

title: 85  Reshaping Mini-Capstone: Change Across Visits
crumbs: 85  Reshaping Mini-Capstone: Change Across Visits

title: 85  Reshaping Mini-Capstone: Change Across Visits
text:
85.5 Interpret the Result
Every county in this subset shows a mean decrease in blood pressure from visit 1 to visit 3, consistent with the mild simulated treatment effect built into this dataset — but the size of that decrease varies meaningfully by county, from Oakford’s modest -1.17 to Springvale’s much larger -10.17. As always (Level 5), the smaller counties’ averages (Cedar Falls and Millbrook, both n=4 after dropout) deserve more caution than Oakford, Riverdale, or Springvale’s more stable n=6 estimates.

Common Mistakes

Computing change before removing missing visit3 values. mutate(change = visit3 - visit1) with a missing visit3 correctly produces NA for change — but if you forget to filter(!is.na(visit3)) (or handle it in summarise() with na.rm = TRUE), those NAs can silently propagate into a NA group mean.
Reshaping all three visits when only two are needed. Including visit2 in the pivot_wider() step doesn’t break anything, but it does create an unused column — filtering down to just the visits you need (as done here) keeps the pipeline focused and avoids confusion.
Interpreting a mean change without checking group size. A -8.00 mean drop in a county of 4 people carries much more uncertainty than a -1.17 mean drop in a county of 6 — reshaping and chaining code correctly is necessary, but not sufficient, for a trustworthy conclusion.
crumbs: 85  Reshaping Mini-Capstone: Change Across Visits

title: 85  Reshaping Mini-Capstone: Change Across Visits
text:
85.6 Practice Problem
Repeat this pipeline for weight instead of sbp (reshape the weight_visit1/weight_visit2/weight_visit3 columns, filter to visit1 and visit3, compute change, and summarise by county). Does weight show a consistent decrease across counties, similar to blood pressure?
crumbs: 85  Reshaping Mini-Capstone: Change Across Visits

title: 85  Reshaping Mini-Capstone: Change Across Visits
text:
85.7 Challenge Problem
Modify the pipeline to compute change between visit1 and visit2 instead of visit1 and visit3. Compare the resulting county rankings — does the county with the largest early change (visit1 to visit2) also have the largest overall change (visit1 to visit3)?
crumbs: 85  Reshaping Mini-Capstone: Change Across Visits

title: 85  Reshaping Mini-Capstone: Change Across Visits
text:
85.8 Key Takeaways

A common reshaping pattern is wide → long (to filter flexibly) → wide again (to align values side by side for a row-wise calculation).
Always resolve missing values from dropout or incomplete follow-up before they propagate into a downstream group summary.
A per-county (or per-group) summary is only as trustworthy as its underlying sample size — reshaping code correctly doesn’t guarantee the resulting numbers deserve equal confidence.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. Why reshape to long format before filtering to specific visits, rather than just selecting wide columns directly?

It’s required by R syntax
It lets you filter by visit as an actual value, which generalizes better than hardcoding column names
Long format uses less memory
There’s no reason; both approaches are identical

True or false. mutate(change = visit3 - visit1) will produce NA for any row where visit3 is missing.
Predict the output. In county_change_summary, which county has the smallest-magnitude mean change (closest to zero)?
Short answer. Why does Cedar Falls’ -8.00 mean change deserve more caution than Oakford’s or Springvale’s figures, even though all three are computed the same way?



Show answers
