b) It lets you filter by visit as an actual value (filter(visit %in% c(...))), which generalizes to any pair of visits rather than hardcoding specific column names.
True. Arithmetic involving a missing value produces NA by default, matching the rule from every earlier level.
Oakford, with a mean change of -1.17 (closest to zero).
Cedar Falls’ mean rests on only 4 participants (this subset’s smallest county), making its average considerably less stable than Oakford’s or Springvale’s, both based on 6 participants.
crumbs: 85  Reshaping Mini-Capstone: Change Across Visits

title: 86  Keys and Relationships
text:
86.1 The R Concept
A key is the column (or combination of columns) that identifies which row in one dataset corresponds to which row in another. Here, id is the key linking baseline_enrollment.csv and followup_visit.csv — every row in each file represents one participant, identified by that same id value.
Two datasets sharing a key can relate to each other in several ways:
Before merging anything, always inspect both key columns first.

title: 86  Keys and Relationships
text:
Every id in both files matches exactly (a clean one-to-one relationship) — rare in real longitudinal data.
Some ids exist in one file but not the other — participants who dropped out before follow-up, or new recruits added at follow-up.
One id in one file matches multiple rows in the other (a one-to-many relationship) — for example, if followup_visit.csv recorded two visits for some participants.

title: 86  Keys and Relationships
text:
86.2 Demonstration
baseline <- read.csv("data/baseline_enrollment.csv")
followup <- read.csv("data/followup_visit.csv")

nrow(baseline)
[1] 20
nrow(followup)
[1] 20
Both files have 20 rows — but that doesn’t mean they contain the same 20 participants.
sum(baseline$id %in% followup$id)
[1] 15
sum(!(baseline$id %in% followup$id))
[1] 5

title: 86  Keys and Relationships
text:
86.3 Explore
Try sum(followup$id %in% baseline$id) and sum(!(followup$id %in% baseline$id)). Compare these counts to the ones above for baseline. Notice the numbers aren’t symmetric in an obvious way until you think it through: some followup ids are genuinely new participants who weren’t in baseline at all.

title: 86  Keys and Relationships
text:
86.4 Solve the Problem
baseline <- read.csv("data/baseline_enrollment.csv")
followup <- read.csv("data/followup_visit.csv")

# ids in baseline but NOT in followup - likely dropouts
setdiff(baseline$id, followup$id)
[1] 16 17 18 19 20
# ids in followup but NOT in baseline - likely new recruits
setdiff(followup$id, baseline$id)
[1] 21 22 23 24 25
# ids present in both files
length(intersect(baseline$id, followup$id))
[1] 15
Exactly as your professor’s description hinted: 5 baseline participants (ids 16-20) have no follow-up record at all — likely dropouts — while 5 follow-up ids (21-25) don’t appear in baseline at all — likely participants recruited after the study began. 15 ids (1-15) appear in both files.

title: 86  Keys and Relationships
text:
86.5 Interpret the Result
Before writing a single join() call, you now know exactly what to expect: a merge that keeps only matching ids will produce 15 rows; a merge that keeps every baseline id will produce 20 (with 5 missing follow-up values); a merge that keeps every id from either file will produce 25. Knowing this ahead of time is exactly what lets you catch a bug if your eventual join produces an unexpected row count.

Common Mistakes

Assuming two files with the same number of rows describe the same units. baseline and followup both happen to have 20 rows here, but they represent partially different sets of 20 participants — row count alone tells you nothing about whether the same individuals are present.
Merging without checking the key column’s uniqueness first. If id had duplicate values within a single file (a one-to-many relationship), a join could produce more rows than either original file — always check length(unique(df$id)) == nrow(df) before assuming a one-to-one relationship.
Not deciding in advance what should happen to non-matching ids. Different join types (next several pages) handle non-matches completely differently — deciding whether you want to keep, drop, or investigate them should happen before you pick which join function to use, not after.

title: 86  Keys and Relationships
text:
86.6 Practice Problem
Use length(unique(baseline$id)) == nrow(baseline) to confirm id is a genuinely unique key in baseline_enrollment.csv (no participant appears twice). Do the same check for followup_visit.csv.

title: 86  Keys and Relationships
text:
86.7 Challenge Problem
Without using any join() function yet, use setdiff() and intersect() to predict, in writing, how many rows each of the following would produce: (a) a merge keeping only ids present in both files, (b) a merge keeping every id from baseline, (c) a merge keeping every id from followup, (d) a merge keeping every id from either file. Confirm your predictions once you reach the following pages.

title: 86  Keys and Relationships
text:
86.8 Key Takeaways

A key is the column that identifies which row in one dataset matches which row in another — here, id.
Two datasets can relate as one-to-one, or with unmatched ids on either side (dropouts, new recruits) — always check both directions with setdiff() before merging.
Predicting the expected row count of a merge before running it is the best way to catch a join gone wrong.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What does setdiff(baseline$id, followup$id) return?

ids present in both files
ids in baseline that are NOT in followup
ids in followup that are NOT in baseline
The total number of participants

True or false. Two data frames with the same number of rows must describe the exact same set of individuals.
Predict the output. Given 20 baseline ids and 20 followup ids, with 15 ids common to both, how many ids are in baseline only?
Short answer. Why is it useful to compute setdiff() and intersect() before running any join function?



Show answers


b) ids in baseline that are NOT in followup.
False. Row count alone says nothing about whether the same individuals are represented — as seen here, both files have 20 rows but only 15 individuals overlap.
5 (20 total baseline ids minus 15 shared with followup).
Knowing the expected overlap ahead of time lets you predict how many rows each join type should produce, so you can immediately catch a mistake if the actual result doesn’t match your prediction.

title: 87  left_join()
text:
87.1 The R Concept
left_join() keeps every row from the first (“left”) data frame, and attaches matching columns from the second (“right”) data frame wherever a match exists — filling in NA where it doesn’t:
The result always has at least as many rows as left_df (exactly as many, unless the key has duplicates on the right side) — nothing from baseline is ever dropped, regardless of whether followup has a matching row.

title: 87  left_join()
text:
left_join(left_df, right_df, by = "key_column")
left_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")

title: 87  left_join()
text:
87.2 Demonstration
library(dplyr)

baseline <- read.csv("data/baseline_enrollment.csv")
followup <- read.csv("data/followup_visit.csv")

combined <- left_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")

nrow(combined)
[1] 20
All 20 baseline participants are present, exactly as many rows as baseline alone had.
combined[combined$id %in% c(16, 17, 18), ]
   id age    sex      county baseline_bmi followup_sbp followup_smoker
16  16  45 Female   Millbrook         30.4           NA              NA
17  17  79 Female  Springvale         21.1           NA              NA
18  18  26   Male     Oakford         29.1           NA              NA
Participants 16, 17, and 18 — who have no follow-up record — still appear in combined, with NA filling in for followup_sbp and followup_smoker.

title: 87  left_join()
text:
87.3 Explore
Try sum(is.na(combined$followup_sbp)). You might expect exactly 5 (the dropout ids 16-20) — but check combined[combined$id == 11, ] too. Participant 11 does have a follow-up record, yet still shows NA for followup_sbp, because that participant’s blood pressure was already missing in the original data (recall Level 4’s messiness) — a pre-existing gap the join carries forward, not a sign the join failed to find a match.

title: 87  left_join()
text:
87.4 Solve the Problem
library(dplyr)

baseline <- read.csv("data/baseline_enrollment.csv")
followup <- read.csv("data/followup_visit.csv")

combined <- left_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")

nrow(combined)
[1] 20
sum(is.na(combined$followup_sbp))
[1] 6
combined[combined$id == 11, c("id", "county", "followup_sbp")]
   id    county followup_sbp
11 11 Riverdale           NA
6, not 5: the 5 true dropout ids (16-20, no matching record at all) plus participant 11, who does have a follow-up row but whose blood pressure reading was already missing in the source data — a pre-existing gap the join simply carries forward, not a sign of a failed match.

title: 87  left_join()
text:
87.5 Interpret the Result
combined now represents every participant your study enrolled, whether or not they returned for follow-up — the true dropouts are visible as fully NA follow-up rows rather than silently vanishing, which is exactly the honest, complete picture your professor needs (a disappearing dropout would understate how many participants were actually lost to follow-up). Participant 11’s single missing reading is a different, narrower kind of gap — present in the original followup_visit.csv before the join ever ran — and worth distinguishing from true loss to follow-up.

Common Mistakes

Confusing “left” with alphabetical or numerical order. “Left” refers to which data frame is listed first in the function call, left_join(baseline, followup, ...) — baseline is “left” here simply because it’s the first argument, not because of anything about its contents.
Forgetting to specify by. Without by = "id", left_join() tries to guess a shared column name automatically — usually correct here, but relying on this guess silently can go wrong if both data frames happen to share an unrelated column name too.
Assuming NA after a join means the join failed. An NA in followup_sbp after left_join() is expected and correct for a participant with no follow-up record — it’s information (this person didn’t return), not an error.

title: 87  left_join()
text:
87.6 Practice Problem
Use left_join(followup, baseline, by = "id") — reversing which data frame is “left.” Compare nrow() of this result to the original left_join(baseline, followup, by = "id"). Are they the same? Why or why not?

title: 87  left_join()
text:
87.7 Challenge Problem
Use left_join() and then filter(!(id %in% followup$id)) (Level 5) to extract just the 5 true dropout participants (ids 16-20) into their own data frame, ready for a “lost to follow-up” report — and explain why filter(is.na(followup_sbp)) alone would incorrectly include participant 11 as well.

title: 87  left_join()
text:
87.8 Key Takeaways

left_join(left_df, right_df, by = "key") keeps every row of left_df, filling in NA for any right-hand columns where no match exists.
“Left” simply means “listed first in the function call” — it has nothing to do with the data’s content or order.
An NA produced by a join is meaningful information (no match found), not necessarily a mistake.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What happens to a row in baseline that has no matching id in followup after left_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")?

It is dropped from the result
It is kept, with NA for the follow-up columns
It causes an error
It is duplicated

True or false. left_join(baseline, followup, ...) and left_join(followup, baseline, ...) always produce the same number of rows.
Predict the output. How many rows would left_join(baseline, followup, by = "id") produce, given 20 baseline participants?
Find the error.
combined <- left_join(baseline, followup)
# unsure whether this correctly used "id" as the join key



Show answers


b) It is kept, with NA filled in for the follow-up columns.
False. left_join() keeps all rows of whichever data frame is listed first — swapping the order can change the row count if the two files have different unmatched-id counts.
20 rows — every baseline participant is retained regardless of follow-up match status.
This isn’t necessarily wrong (left_join() will guess a shared column automatically), but it’s fragile style — always specify by = "id" explicitly, so the join key is unambiguous and documented in the code itself.

title: 88  inner_join()
text:
88.1 The R Concept
inner_join() keeps only the rows whose key exists in both data frames — any row without a match on either side is dropped entirely, so the join itself introduces no new NAs (though NAs already present in either file’s own columns, from earlier missingness, still come along for the ride):
Unlike left_join(), inner_join()’s result can have fewer rows than either original data frame — exactly the count of ids common to both.

title: 88  inner_join()
text:
inner_join(df1, df2, by = "key_column")
inner_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")

title: 88  inner_join()
text:
88.2 Demonstration
library(dplyr)

baseline <- read.csv("data/baseline_enrollment.csv")
followup <- read.csv("data/followup_visit.csv")

complete_cases <- inner_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")

nrow(complete_cases)
[1] 15
15 rows — exactly the number of ids found in both files with intersect() two pages ago.
sum(is.na(complete_cases))
[1] 3
Not zero — inner_join() guarantees every remaining row has a matching id on both sides, but it does nothing to clean up missingness that already existed within either file’s own columns (recall baseline_bmi and age each had one pre-existing missing value from Level 3-4’s messiness). The join only controls whether rows appear at all, not whether every field inside them is complete.

title: 88  inner_join()
text:
88.3 Explore
Try range(complete_cases$id). Confirm the ids present are exactly 1 through 15 — the ids identified as common to both files — with none of the dropouts (16-20) or new recruits (21-25) appearing anywhere in the result.

title: 88  inner_join()
text:
88.4 Solve the Problem
library(dplyr)

baseline <- read.csv("data/baseline_enrollment.csv")
followup <- read.csv("data/followup_visit.csv")

complete_cases <- inner_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")

nrow(complete_cases)
[1] 15
cor(complete_cases$baseline_bmi, complete_cases$followup_sbp, use = "complete.obs")
[1] -0.1682
15 participant records with matching baseline and follow-up ids, ready for your professor’s comparison — use = "complete.obs" (Level 11 preview) further narrows the correlation itself to the 13 participants with both a non-missing baseline_bmi and followup_sbp, showing a weak negative correlation not worth drawing strong conclusions from at this sample size.

title: 88  inner_join()
text:
88.5 Interpret the Result
complete_cases intentionally excludes the 5 dropouts (no follow-up record at all) and the 5 new recruits (no baseline record at all) — leaving only participants where a genuine before-and-after comparison is possible. It doesn’t, however, guarantee every column within those 15 rows is filled in — id10’s baseline_bmi and id13’s age remain NA, exactly as they were in the original files, a reminder that matching keys and complete data are two separate concerns. It’s also worth remembering who got excluded by the join itself, since dropouts are rarely random (Level 12 will return to this idea).

Common Mistakes

Using inner_join() when you actually wanted to preserve everyone. If your goal was “show me the full enrolled cohort, follow-up or not,” inner_join() silently discards the very dropout information left_join() would have preserved — pick the join type based on what you actually need to see.
Not reporting how many rows were dropped. Going from 20 baseline participants to 15 complete cases is itself an important finding (25% attrition) — an inner_join() without also reporting the original counts can hide a meaningful loss of data from a reader.
Assuming inner_join() and left_join() differ only when there’s missing data. If every key matched perfectly in both directions, inner_join() and left_join() would return identical results — the difference only becomes visible once you have non-matching rows, exactly the case here.
Assuming inner_join() guarantees a fully complete dataset. inner_join() only guarantees every remaining row has a matching key on both sides — it says nothing about whether the individual columns within those matched rows are themselves free of NA, as id10’s missing baseline_bmi shows here.

title: 88  inner_join()
text:
88.6 Practice Problem
Compute nrow(baseline) - nrow(complete_cases) and confirm it equals the 5 dropouts identified two pages ago with setdiff().

title: 88  inner_join()
text:
88.7 Challenge Problem
Use inner_join() and then group_by(county) + summarise() (Level 5) to compute the mean followup_sbp for each county among only the 15 complete-case participants. Compare this to what you’d get if you mistakenly used all 20 baseline rows via left_join() and forgot to handle the NAs.

title: 88  inner_join()
text:
88.8 Key Takeaways

inner_join(df1, df2, by = "key") keeps only rows whose key exists in both data frames — no NAs are produced, but rows without a match are silently dropped.
inner_join()’s result can have fewer rows than either input, exactly the count of matching keys.
Always report how many rows a join dropped — an inner_join()’s missing participants are themselves often an important finding (like attrition), not just a technical detail.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What does inner_join(baseline, followup, by = "id") keep?

Every row from baseline, regardless of a match
Every row from followup, regardless of a match
Only rows whose id exists in both data frames
Every row from either data frame

True or false. inner_join() can never produce a result containing any NA values.
Predict the output. Given 20 baseline ids and 15 common to both files, how many rows would inner_join(baseline, followup, by = "id") produce?
Short answer. Why might reporting only the inner_join() result, without mentioning the original 20 baseline participants, be misleading in a study report?



Show answers


c) Only rows whose id exists in both data frames.
False. inner_join() prevents new NAs from being introduced by non-matching keys, but NAs already present in either file’s own columns before the join (like id10’s missing baseline_bmi) still appear in the result.
15 rows — exactly the ids common to both baseline and followup.
It would hide the fact that 5 of the original 20 enrolled participants (25%) had no follow-up data at all — an attrition rate that’s often clinically and statistically important to report alongside any complete-case analysis.

title: 89  right_join() and full_join()
text:
89.1 The R Concept
right_join() is left_join() with the roles reversed — it keeps every row of the second data frame, filling in NA for any first-data-frame columns that don’t have a match:
full_join() goes further still, keeping every row from both data frames — matched where possible, NA-filled on whichever side lacks a match:

title: 89  right_join() and full_join()
text:
right_join(df1, df2, by = "key_column")
# equivalent to:
left_join(df2, df1, by = "key_column")   # with column order preserved differently
right_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")

full_join(df1, df2, by = "key_column")
full_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")

title: 89  right_join() and full_join()
text:
89.2 Demonstration
library(dplyr)

baseline <- read.csv("data/baseline_enrollment.csv")
followup <- read.csv("data/followup_visit.csv")

right_result <- right_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")

nrow(right_result)
[1] 20
right_result[right_result$id %in% c(21, 22), ]
   id age  sex county baseline_bmi followup_sbp followup_smoker
21  21  NA <NA>   <NA>           NA          129             Yes
22  22  NA <NA>   <NA>           NA          124              No
Participants 21 and 22 — new recruits with no baseline record — still appear, with NA filling in every baseline column.
full_result <- full_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")

nrow(full_result)
[1] 25

title: 89  right_join() and full_join()
text:
89.3 Explore
Try sort(unique(full_result$id)). Confirm it runs from 1 to 25 with no gaps — every id that appeared in either file is present exactly once, whether it was a dropout, a new recruit, or a complete case.

title: 89  right_join() and full_join()
text:
89.4 Solve the Problem
library(dplyr)

baseline <- read.csv("data/baseline_enrollment.csv")
followup <- read.csv("data/followup_visit.csv")

right_result <- right_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")
nrow(right_result)
[1] 20
sum(is.na(right_result$baseline_bmi))
[1] 6
full_result <- full_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")
nrow(full_result)
[1] 25
right_join() produces 20 rows (every follow-up participant), with 6 missing baseline_bmi values — the 5 genuinely new recruits (21-25) plus participant 10, whose baseline BMI was already missing in the source data. full_join() produces 25 rows — the complete union of every id that appeared anywhere across both files.

title: 89  right_join() and full_join()
text:
89.5 Interpret the Result
right_result answers “who showed up at follow-up, and what do we know about them from baseline (if anything)?” — useful when the follow-up file is your primary population of interest. full_result answers a broader question: “who was ever part of this study, at any point?” — the union of all 25 distinct participants across both files, useful for a complete accounting of the whole study’s reach, dropouts and new recruits alike.

Common Mistakes

Forgetting right_join(df1, df2) and left_join(df2, df1) aren’t quite identical. They keep the same rows, but the resulting column order differs (whichever data frame is listed first keeps its columns first) — the row-level result is equivalent, but the layout isn’t automatically identical.
Reaching for full_join() by default “just to be safe.” full_join() guarantees no one is excluded, but it also guarantees more NAs to handle — if your analysis genuinely needs complete cases (Page 10’s inner_join()), a full_join() just adds cleanup work rather than solving the problem.
Losing track of which NAs mean “no matching record” versus “record exists but this specific field was missing.” As seen throughout this level, both kinds of NA can appear in the same joined column, and conflating them can lead to miscounting genuine non-participation versus ordinary data entry gaps.

title: 89  right_join() and full_join()
text:
89.6 Practice Problem
Use right_join(baseline, followup, by = "id") and then filter(is.na(age)) (Level 5) to extract the new recruits who have no baseline record. How many are there, and does that match the 5 identified with setdiff() on the keys-and-relationships page?

title: 89  right_join() and full_join()
text:
89.7 Challenge Problem
Compute nrow(full_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")) using only nrow(baseline), nrow(followup), and the intersect()/setdiff() counts from the keys-and-relationships page (hint: total unique ids = baseline count + followup count − ids common to both). Confirm your formula matches the actual full_join() row count.

title: 89  right_join() and full_join()
text:
89.8 Key Takeaways

right_join(df1, df2, by = "key") keeps every row of df2, mirroring left_join() with the roles reversed.
full_join(df1, df2, by = "key") keeps every row from either data frame — the complete union of both files’ ids.
Choose the join type based on which population you actually want represented in the result, not by defaulting to whichever seems “safest.”


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What does right_join(baseline, followup, by = "id") keep?

Every row from baseline
Every row from followup
Only rows present in both
No rows — it always errors

True or false. full_join() can produce a result with more rows than either original data frame alone.
Predict the output. Given 20 baseline ids, 20 followup ids, and 15 common to both, how many rows would full_join(baseline, followup, by = "id") produce?
Short answer. Why might right_join() be more useful than left_join() if your analysis is primarily about the follow-up visit itself, rather than the original enrolled cohort?



Show answers


b) Every row from followup.
True. full_join()’s row count equals the number of distinct ids across both files combined — which can exceed either individual file’s row count whenever there are any non-matching ids on either side.
25 rows (20 + 20 − 15 common = 25 distinct ids total).
right_join() centers the result on the follow-up population (including new recruits not in baseline), whereas left_join() would center it on the original baseline cohort (including dropouts) — the right choice depends on which group is the actual subject of the analysis.

title: 90  semi_join() and anti_join()
text:
90.1 The R Concept
semi_join() keeps only the rows of the first data frame that have a matching key in the second — but adds none of the second data frame’s columns:
anti_join() does the opposite: it keeps only the rows of the first data frame that do not have a matching key in the second — again, adding no new columns:
Both are filtering joins — unlike left_join()/inner_join()/right_join()/full_join() (all “mutating joins” that add columns), semi_join() and anti_join() only ever return a subset of the first data frame’s own rows and columns.

title: 90  semi_join() and anti_join()
text:
semi_join(df1, df2, by = "key_column")
semi_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")

anti_join(df1, df2, by = "key_column")
anti_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")

title: 90  semi_join() and anti_join()
text:
90.2 Demonstration
library(dplyr)

baseline <- read.csv("data/baseline_enrollment.csv")
followup <- read.csv("data/followup_visit.csv")

has_followup <- semi_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")

nrow(has_followup)
[1] 15
names(has_followup)
[1] "id"           "age"          "sex"          "county"       "baseline_bmi"
15 rows — the participants with a follow-up match — but notice: only baseline’s own 5 columns, with nothing from followup attached (unlike inner_join(), which would have added followup_sbp and followup_smoker too).
lost_to_followup <- anti_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")

lost_to_followup$id
[1] 16 17 18 19 20

title: 90  semi_join() and anti_join()
text:
90.3 Explore
Try anti_join(followup, baseline, by = "id") — reversing which file is first. Confirm this returns the 5 new recruits (ids 21-25) instead of the 5 dropouts — anti_join()’s result depends entirely on which data frame is listed first, exactly like every join so far.

title: 90  semi_join() and anti_join()
text:
90.4 Solve the Problem
library(dplyr)

baseline <- read.csv("data/baseline_enrollment.csv")
followup <- read.csv("data/followup_visit.csv")

lost_to_followup <- anti_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")
new_recruits <- anti_join(followup, baseline, by = "id")

lost_to_followup[, c("id", "county", "age")]
   id      county age
16 16   Millbrook  45
17 17  Springvale  79
18 18     Oakford  26
19 19 Cedar Falls  80
20 20     Oakford  55
new_recruits$id
[1] 21 22 23 24 25
Two clean, separate reports for your professor: exactly the 5 baseline participants lost to follow-up (ids 16-20, ages ranging from 26 to 80), and exactly the 5 new recruits who joined only at follow-up (ids 21-25) — each extracted with a single filtering join, no unwanted columns attached.

title: 90  semi_join() and anti_join()
text:
90.5 Interpret the Result
semi_join() and anti_join() answer “which rows match (or don’t)?” without the complexity of merged, NA-filled columns — often exactly what’s needed when the goal is identifying who falls into a group, not analyzing their combined data. Compare this to Page 10’s inner_join(), which answers a related but different question (“give me the matched data, combined”) by adding columns rather than just filtering rows.

Common Mistakes

Reaching for inner_join() when you only needed a filter. If your goal is just “which participants match,” semi_join() gives a cleaner result than inner_join() followed by manually dropping the unwanted follow-up columns.
Forgetting anti_join()’s direction matters enormously. anti_join(baseline, followup, ...) (dropouts) and anti_join(followup, baseline, ...) (new recruits) answer completely different questions — mixing them up reverses which group you’re actually reporting on.
Expecting semi_join() to add any columns at all. Unlike every other join in this level, semi_join()’s result never has more columns than its first argument — if you need followup’s columns too, semi_join() is the wrong tool; reach for inner_join() instead.

title: 90  semi_join() and anti_join()
text:
90.6 Practice Problem
Use semi_join(followup, baseline, by = "id") to find which follow-up participants also have a baseline record. How many are there, and does this match inner_join()’s row count from Page 10?

title: 90  semi_join() and anti_join()
text:
90.7 Challenge Problem
Confirm that nrow(semi_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")) + nrow(anti_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")) equals nrow(baseline) exactly. Explain in words why this must always be true for any two data frames and any key column.

title: 90  semi_join() and anti_join()
text:
90.8 Key Takeaways

semi_join(df1, df2, by = "key") keeps only df1’s rows that have a match in df2, with none of df2’s columns added.
anti_join(df1, df2, by = "key") keeps only df1’s rows that have no match in df2 — again, no new columns.
Both are filtering joins, distinct from the “mutating joins” (left_join(), inner_join(), right_join(), full_join()) that combine columns from both data frames.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. How many columns does semi_join(baseline, followup, by = "id") return, if baseline has 5 columns and followup has 3?

8 (5 + 3)
5 (only baseline’s columns)
3 (only followup’s columns)
2 (only the shared key and one other)

True or false. anti_join(baseline, followup, by = "id") and anti_join(followup, baseline, by = "id") return the same rows.
Predict the output. Given 20 baseline ids and 15 with a follow-up match, how many rows would anti_join(baseline, followup, by = "id") return?
Short answer. When would you prefer semi_join() over inner_join(), even though both only keep matching rows?



Show answers


b) 5 — semi_join() never adds columns from the second data frame, regardless of how many it has.
False. The two calls answer different questions entirely — dropouts (baseline-only) versus new recruits (followup-only) — and generally return completely different rows.
5 rows — the baseline ids (16-20) with no matching follow-up record.
When you only need to know which rows match, not to actually combine data from both files — semi_join() avoids adding unwanted columns that you’d otherwise have to select() away after an inner_join().

title: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together
text:
91.1 The R Concept
Real analyses frequently need both reshaping and joining — reshape one dataset into an analysis-ready shape, then join in additional variables from an entirely different source before the real analysis can happen:
crumbs: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together

title: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together
crumbs: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together

title: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together
text:
91.2 Demonstration
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

bp_wide <- read.csv("data/bp_visits_wide.csv")
baseline <- read.csv("data/baseline_enrollment.csv")

bp_change <- bp_wide |>
  pivot_longer(cols = starts_with("sbp_visit"), names_to = "visit", names_prefix = "sbp_", values_to = "sbp") |>
  filter(visit %in% c("visit1", "visit3")) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = visit, values_from = sbp) |>
  filter(!is.na(visit3)) |>
  mutate(change = visit3 - visit1)

nrow(bp_change)
[1] 26
bp_change_with_age <- left_join(bp_change, baseline[, c("id", "age")], by = "id")

sum(is.na(bp_change_with_age$age))
[1] 10
10 of the 26 complete-case participants have no usable age — 9 simply aren’t in baseline_enrollment.csv at all (this visits study enrolled a broader group than the baseline file covers), and 1 (participant 13) is in baseline_enrollment.csv but has a missing age value there.
crumbs: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together

title: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together
text:
91.3 Explore
Try filter(bp_change_with_age, is.na(age)) and inspect which ids these are. Confirm 9 of them are ids outside baseline_enrollment.csv’s range entirely (using setdiff()), and 1 (id 13) is inside the range but has its own pre-existing missing age.
crumbs: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together

title: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together
crumbs: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together

title: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together
text:
91.5 Interpret the Result
Answering your professor’s question required chaining nearly every tool from this level: pivot_longer() and pivot_wider() to align visit1 and visit3 for a per-participant change score, filter() to handle dropouts (Page 7), and finally left_join() to bring in age from a completely separate file — with 10 participants necessarily excluded from the final age-based comparison because that information simply doesn’t exist for them. The under-50 group’s larger average blood pressure drop is a genuine pattern in this small dataset, but with 7 and 9 participants per group, it’s a hypothesis to investigate further (Level 11), not a settled conclusion.

Common Mistakes

Forgetting that joining across studies often means partial coverage, not full coverage. bp_visits_wide.csv and baseline_enrollment.csv weren’t guaranteed to describe the same participants — 9 of 26 simply had no baseline record at all, which is normal and expected, not a data error to “fix.”
Silently dropping unmatched rows without reporting how many. The move from 26 complete-case participants to 16 with usable age is a meaningful reduction — always state it explicitly rather than letting the final summary table’s row count speak for itself.
Treating a striking small-sample pattern as a confirmed finding. The under-50 group’s larger blood pressure drop, based on just 9 participants, is worth flagging and investigating further — not yet reported as if it were a validated result.
crumbs: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together

title: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together
text:
91.6 Practice Problem
Redo this pipeline joining in sex instead of age from baseline_enrollment.csv, and compare mean blood pressure change between male and female participants (among those with a usable sex value).
crumbs: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together

title: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together
text:
91.7 Challenge Problem
Redo the join using full_join() instead of left_join() between bp_change and baseline. Does nrow() of the result change? Explain what full_join() would add here that left_join() didn’t, given that baseline might contain enrolled participants who aren’t in bp_visits_wide.csv at all.
crumbs: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together

title: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together
text:
91.8 Key Takeaways

Real analyses often require reshaping and joining together — reshaping to compute a per-unit value, then joining to bring in variables that weren’t part of the original file.
Joining across two independently collected files frequently produces partial, not full, coverage — always report how many rows lack a match, and why that’s expected rather than an error.
A striking pattern found in a small, joined subset should be reported as a lead worth investigating, not treated as a final answer.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. Why was left_join() (not inner_join()) used to bring age into bp_change?

left_join() runs faster
To keep every complete-case visits participant, even those without a baseline match, rather than silently dropping them
inner_join() doesn’t exist in dplyr
There is no difference between the two here

True or false. Every participant in bp_visits_wide.csv also appears in baseline_enrollment.csv.
Predict the output. If 26 participants have complete visit data and 10 lack a usable age, how many remain in the final age-group summary?
Short answer. Why should the under-50 group’s larger average blood pressure drop be reported cautiously, even though the calculation itself is correct?



Show answers


b) To keep every complete-case visits participant, even those without a baseline match, rather than silently dropping them (an inner_join() would have removed them immediately, hiding the coverage gap).
False. 9 of the 26 complete-case participants have no matching id in baseline_enrollment.csv at all.
16 participants (26 minus the 10 lacking a usable age).
The comparison rests on only 7 (50+) and 9 (under 50) participants — small enough that the pattern could easily be due to chance rather than a real, generalizable effect; it’s worth investigating further, not yet reporting as a confirmed finding.
crumbs: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together

title: Level 7 — Descriptive Statistics
text:
A preview mission
Your mission across this level: write the first paragraph of a health report — the “Table 1” description of your study sample that every published study opens with — entirely from numbers you compute yourself, correctly handling the missing values that have run through this dataset since Level 1.

title: 92  Mean, Median, and Mode
text:
92.1 The R Concept
The mean is the sum of all values divided by how many there are — sensitive to unusually large or small values (“outliers”). The median is the middle value once the data is sorted (or the average of the two middle values, for an even count) — much less affected by outliers. The mode is the most frequently occurring value:
R has no built-in function for the statistical mode. The function called mode() in base R does something completely unrelated — it reports an object’s storage type ("numeric", "character", etc.), not its most frequent value:
That’s age’s storage type, not its most common value — a well-known trap for anyone coming from other statistical software. Finding the actual statistical mode requires table() (Level 4) instead:

title: 92  Mean, Median, and Mode
text:
mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)
median(x, na.rm = TRUE)

mode(participants$age)
[1] "numeric"

table(x)[which.max(table(x))]

title: 92  Mean, Median, and Mode
text:
92.2 Demonstration
participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

mean(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE)
[1] 47.42553
median(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE)
[1] 49
The mean (47.43) and median (49) are reasonably close here, suggesting age isn’t wildly skewed by extreme outliers — though they’re not identical, so it’s still worth reporting both.
mode(participants$age)
[1] "numeric"
Exactly the trap described above — mode() reports the storage type, telling you nothing about which age value is most common.

title: 92  Mean, Median, and Mode
text:
92.3 Explore
Try table(participants$age) directly and scan the output for the largest count. Then try sort(table(participants$age), decreasing = TRUE)[1:3] to see the three most frequent ages at a glance, without scanning the whole table by eye.

title: 92  Mean, Median, and Mode
text:
92.4 Solve the Problem
participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

mean_age <- mean(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE)
median_age <- median(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE)

age_table <- table(participants$age)
mode_age <- names(age_table)[which.max(age_table)]

mean_age
[1] 47.42553
median_age
[1] 49
mode_age
[1] "53"
age_table[mode_age]
53
 5
Mean age 47.43, median age 49, and the single most common age is 53 (occurring 5 times) — three different, complementary answers to “what’s typical,” none of which is simply “more correct” than the others.

title: 92  Mean, Median, and Mode
text:
92.5 Interpret the Result
The mean pulls slightly lower than the median here, hinting at a slight leftward skew (a few younger participants pulling the average down relative to the middle value) — consistent with what you’d expect from a health study spanning ages 18 to 81. The mode (53) tells a different kind of story: it doesn’t describe “the middle” or “the average,” just which single value happens to repeat most often — useful for categorical data, but often less informative than the mean or median for a wide-ranging numeric variable like age.

Common Mistakes

Using mode() expecting the statistical mode. As demonstrated above, mode() in R reports an object’s storage type — this is one of the most common traps for beginners, especially those coming from Excel or other statistical software where “mode” always means “most frequent value.”
Forgetting na.rm = TRUE for mean and median alike. Both mean() and median() return NA by default if any value is missing — the Level 2 rule applies to both identically, not just to mean().
Assuming mean and median should always be close together. They coincide closely for roughly symmetric data, but can diverge substantially for skewed data (like income, where a few very high earners pull the mean well above the median) — a difference between them is itself useful information, not an error.

title: 92  Mean, Median, and Mode
text:
92.6 Practice Problem
Compute the mean, median, and mode of bmi. Are the mean and median close together, or do they diverge notably? What might that suggest about the shape of the BMI distribution?

title: 92  Mean, Median, and Mode
text:
92.7 Challenge Problem
Write your own small function (Level 9 preview) called get_mode() that takes a vector and returns its most frequent value using table() and which.max(), handling NA values appropriately. Test it on participants$county (a categorical variable, where “mode” is a much more natural summary than it is for age).

title: 92  Mean, Median, and Mode
text:
92.8 Key Takeaways

Mean, median, and mode each describe “what’s typical” differently — mean is sensitive to outliers, median is not, and mode identifies the single most frequent value.
R’s built-in mode() function reports storage type, not the statistical mode — use table() combined with which.max() instead.
A meaningful difference between mean and median can itself be an informative finding about a variable’s distribution shape.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What does mode(participants$age) actually return in base R?

The most frequent age
The middle age value
The storage type of the age column (e.g., "numeric")
An error, since mode() doesn’t exist

True or false. median() is generally less sensitive to extreme outliers than mean().
Predict the output. If age has 94 non-missing values (an even count), how is the median calculated?
Find the error.
most_common_age <- mode(participants$age)



Show answers


c) The storage type of the age column — mode() in base R is unrelated to the statistical mode.
True. The median only depends on the middle value(s) once sorted, so extreme values don’t pull it the way they pull the mean.
As the average of the two middle values once all 94 non-missing ages are sorted.
mode() doesn’t compute the statistical mode in R — it returns the object’s storage type. Finding the actual most frequent age requires table(participants$age) combined with which.max().
